Title: Magma Emblem

The Magma Emblem (Japanese: マグマのしるし Magma Symbol) is a Key Item introduced in Generation III. The Magma Emblem allows the player to open the hidden entrance to Team Magma's Hideout at the Jagged Pass.

Title: Old Sea Map

The Old Sea Map (Japanese: ふるびたかいず Old Sea Chart) is an event-exclusive Key Item exclusive to Pokémon Emerald. It is needed to access Faraway Island, a location at which Mew can be encountered and caught. The Old Sea Map allows the player to travel to Faraway Island via the S.S. Tidal, where a wild Mew can be encountered. When the player has the Old Sea Map, if they interact with the woman standing in front of the S.S. Tidal at the Lilycove City Harbor, she will fetch the captain. The captain initially claims it is impossible to take the player to the area denoted on the chart, but Mr. Briney scolds him for turning the player away and volunteers to take the player to Faraway Island, even though it is a long way from Hoenn. After the boat ride, the player enters Faraway Island, a small island with a thick patch of forest. Deep within this forest, the player notices Mew, who flees playfully. After a short game of hide and seek, the player is able to pin Mew in a corner and engage it in battle. Main article: List of other event distributions in Generation III → Old Sea Map The Old Sea Map was only distributed in Japan and Taiwan, both for the Japanese version of Pokémon Emerald. In Japan, it was available at Pokémon Festa from July 17 to August 21, 2005, as well as at PokéPark from September 1 to 25, 2005. In Taiwan, it was available at PokéPark from June 23 to September 24, 2006 for people with a Pokémon Download Qualification Certificate (Chinese: 神奇寶貝下載資格證), which was distributed with each purchase of a ticket to the movie Lucario and the Mystery of Mew. The Old Sea Map was never distributed for other language versions. Instead, Mew was distributed directly in several other events for the Generation III games. The Old Sea Map was one of the items Ultima stole from Team Rocket's stash of rare items at Trainer Tower in Bested by Banette. She had been looking for it in order to help her old friend, Mr. Briney, find a way to Faraway Island. When Ultima called Briney about her find, however, she was shocked to learn that the old sailor had managed to find the island without the map. The Old Sea Map and the related event were both created by Shigeki Morimoto, Mew's original designer.

Title: Douse Drive

The Douse Drive (Japanese: アクアカセット Aqua Cassette) is a type of held item introduced in Generation V. It is one of the Drives, which are associated with Genesect. It corresponds to the Water type. If the holder uses Techno Blast, it becomes a Water-type move. Changes a Genesect holding this item to its Douse Drive form. Item-manipulating effects (such as Trick and Fling) cannot give Genesect a Drive or remove a Drive from Genesect. Changes a Genesect holding this item to its Douse Drive form. The Douse Drive, along with the other Drives, appeared in the movie Genesect and the Legend Awakened, attached to a member of the Genesect Army. While it was used with the same purpose of changing the type of Techno Blast in the movie, it was also the key item to tell its owner apart from the other Genesect. The Genesect with the Douse Drive had a different personality from the others, and was typically timid and kind unless forced to act by its leader. In Innocent Scientist, the Douse Drive, along with the other Drives, was under Colress's possession.

Title: Shock Drive

The Shock Drive (Japanese: イナズマカセット Lightning Cassette) is a type of held item introduced in Generation V. It is one of the Drives, which are associated with Genesect. It corresponds to the Electric type. If the holder uses Techno Blast, it becomes an Electric-type move. Changes a Genesect holding this item to its Shock Drive form. Item-manipulating effects (such as Trick and Fling) cannot give Genesect a Drive or remove a Drive from Genesect. Changes a Genesect holding this item to its Shock Drive form. The Shock Drive, along with the other Drives, appeared in the movie Genesect and the Legend Awakened, attached to a member of the Genesect Army. While it was used with the same purpose of changing the type of Techno Blast in the movie, it was also the key item to tell its owner apart from the other Genesect. From Mr. Perfect to Innocent Scientist, Colress used a Genesect to battle against Blake. After falling for one of Blake's taunts, Colress angrily switched the Drive on Genesect's back for a Shock Drive in order to put Blake's Dewott at a disadvantage against the Paleozoic Pokémon. Once Blake had defeated and captured Genesect, he obtained the Shock Drive in the process. The Shock Drive was later given to the Magician in order to reverse-engineer another set of Burn, Chill, and Douse Drives.

Title: Burn Drive

The Burn Drive (Japanese: ブレイズカセット Blaze Cassette) is a type of held item introduced in Generation V. It is one of the Drives, which are associated with Genesect. It corresponds to the Fire type. If the holder uses Techno Blast, it becomes a Fire-type move. Changes a Genesect holding this item to its Burn Drive form. Item-manipulating effects (such as Trick and Fling) cannot give Genesect a Drive or remove a Drive from Genesect. Changes a Genesect holding this item to its Burn Drive form. The Burn Drive, along with the other Drives, appeared in the movie Genesect and the Legend Awakened, attached to a member of the Genesect Army. While it was used with the same purpose of changing the type of Techno Blast in the movie, it was also the key item to tell its owner apart from the other Genesect. From Mr. Perfect to Innocent Scientist, Colress used a Genesect to battle against Blake. Once Blake had defeated and captured Genesect, he obtained the Shock Drive in the process. The Shock Drive was later given to the Magician in order to reverse-engineer another set of Burn, Chill, and Douse Drives. Blake used the Burn Drive to battle Team Plasma on the Plasma Frigate, though, due to it being incomplete at the time, Genesect was injured by its own attack and left exhausted after using Techno Blast on some Team Plasma Grunts. After Blake had later used the Fire-type Techno Blast to save the Swords of Justice from being frozen in ice, Cobalion knocked the damaged Burn Drive off of Genesect's body, after which it exploded.

Title: Chill Drive

The Chill Drive (Japanese: フリーズカセット Freeze Cassette) is a type of held item introduced in Generation V. It is one of the Drives, which are associated with Genesect. It corresponds to the Ice type. If the holder uses Techno Blast, it becomes an Ice-type move. Changes a Genesect holding this item to its Chill Drive form. Item-manipulating effects (such as Trick and Fling) cannot give Genesect a Drive or remove a Drive from Genesect. Changes a Genesect holding this item to its Chill Drive form. The Chill Drive, along with the other Drives, appeared in the movie Genesect and the Legend Awakened, attached to a member of the Genesect Army. While it was used with the same purpose of changing the type of Techno Blast in the movie, it was also the key item to tell its owner apart from the other Genesect. In Innocent Scientist, the Chill Drive, along with the other Drives, was under Colress's possession.

Title: Sweet Heart

The Sweet Heart (Japanese: ハートスイーツ Heart Sweets) is a type of medicine introduced in Generation V. It is a heart-shaped chocolate that restores a Pokémon's HP. In the Generation V games, the Sweet Heart can be sold to the gourmet maniac on Route 5 for $100. When used from the Bag on a Pokémon, it heals the Pokémon by 20 HP. In Mistralton City, the player can give a woman ten Sweet Hearts in exchange for a Heart Scale. In Pokémon Black 2 and White 2, on Valentine's Day (February 14) and White Day (March 14), the woman will allow the exchange for only five Sweet Hearts. In Pride of a Champion!, James's Morpeko was seen eating a Sweet Heart. Sweet Hearts are based on heart-shaped chocolates which are a popular gift between lovers. Their name is a pun on the term of endearment "sweetheart".

Title: Greet Mail

The Greet Mail (Japanese: はじめてメール Greeting Mail) is a type of Mail exclusive to the Generation V games. It contains a message with up to three sentence slots which can be filled with words and phrases using the predefined list from the easy chat system. This was designed to be readable in games of any language. The available sentences for Greet Mail are: The player writes the message when giving this item to be held by a Pokémon. The message can be sent to other games of the same generation by trading the Pokémon holding this item. The message remains available as long as this item is held by a Pokémon or stored in the Mailbox. This item is unable to have any message when stored in the Bag or in the Item Storage System, but there is the option to delete any existing message when storing this item in these places. Once the message is deleted from this item, the player is able to reuse it with a new message. A Pokémon holding this item cannot be placed in the PC or in the Pokémon Day Care. As with other held items, a Pokémon holding this item cannot be transferred into Generation VI games through the Poké Transporter and the Pokémon Bank. This item can be removed by Knock Off. However, it cannot be stolen by Covet, Thief, or the Pickpocket Ability, it causes Bestow and Fling to fail when used by the holder, and causes Switcheroo or Trick to fail when used by or on the holder.

Title: Favored Mail

The Favored Mail (Japanese: だいすきメール Loveable Mail) is a type of Mail exclusive to the Generation V games. It contains a message with up to three sentence slots which can be filled with words and phrases using the predefined list from the easy chat system. This was designed to be readable in games of any language. The available sentences for Favored Mail are: It's great because it's <word 1>. Don't you agree? The player writes the message when giving this item to be held by a Pokémon. The message can be sent to other games of the same generation by trading the Pokémon holding this item. The message remains available as long as this item is held by a Pokémon or stored in the Mailbox. This item is unable to have any message when stored in the Bag or in the Item Storage System, but there is the option to delete any existing message when storing this item in these places. Once the message is deleted from this item, the player is able to reuse it with a new message. A Pokémon holding this item cannot be placed in the PC or in the Pokémon Day Care. As with other held items, a Pokémon holding this item cannot be transferred into Generation VI games through the Poké Transporter and the Pokémon Bank. This item can be removed by Knock Off. However, it cannot be stolen by Covet, Thief, or the Pickpocket Ability, it causes Bestow and Fling to fail when used by the holder, and causes Switcheroo or Trick to fail when used by or on the holder.

Title: Thanks Mail

The Thanks Mail (Japanese: かんしゃメール Gratitude Mail) is a type of Mail exclusive to the Generation V games. It contains a message with up to three sentence slots which can be filled with words and phrases using the predefined list from the easy chat system. This was designed to be readable in games of any language. The available sentences for Thanks Mail are: The player writes the message when giving this item to be held by a Pokémon. The message can be sent to other games of the same generation by trading the Pokémon holding this item. The message remains available as long as this item is held by a Pokémon or stored in the Mailbox. This item is unable to have any message when stored in the Bag or in the Item Storage System, but there is the option to delete any existing message when storing this item in these places. Once the message is deleted from this item, the player is able to reuse it with a new message. A Pokémon holding this item cannot be placed in the PC or in the Pokémon Day Care. As with other held items, a Pokémon holding this item cannot be transferred into Generation VI games through the Poké Transporter and the Pokémon Bank. This item can be removed by Knock Off. However, it cannot be stolen by Covet, Thief, or the Pickpocket Ability, it causes Bestow and Fling to fail when used by the holder, and causes Switcheroo or Trick to fail when used by or on the holder. It depicts Shaymin, and its Japanese name is a direct reference to Shaymin’s Pokédex classification, namely the "Gratitude Pokémon."

Title: Inquiry Mail

The Inquiry Mail (Japanese: しつもんメール Enquiry Mail) is a type of Mail exclusive to the Generation V games. It contains a message with up to three sentence slots which can be filled with words and phrases using the predefined list from the easy chat system. This was designed to be readable in games of any language. The available sentences for Inquiry Mail are: It bothers us, doesn't it? I'm talking about <word 1>. The player writes the message when giving this item to be held by a Pokémon. The message can be sent to other games of the same generation by trading the Pokémon holding this item. The message remains available as long as this item is held by a Pokémon or stored in the Mailbox. This item is unable to have any message when stored in the Bag or in the Item Storage System, but there is the option to delete any existing message when storing this item in these places. Once the message is deleted from this item, the player is able to reuse it with a new message. A Pokémon holding this item cannot be placed in the PC or in the Pokémon Day Care. As with other held items, a Pokémon holding this item cannot be transferred into Generation VI games through the Poké Transporter and the Pokémon Bank. This item can be removed by Knock Off. However, it cannot be stolen by Covet, Thief, or the Pickpocket Ability, it causes Bestow and Fling to fail when used by the holder, and causes Switcheroo or Trick to fail when used by or on the holder.

Title: Like Mail

The Like Mail (Japanese: おすすめメール Recommendation Mail) is a type of Mail exclusive to the Generation V games. It contains a message with up to three sentence slots which can be filled with words and phrases using the predefined list from the easy chat system. This was designed to be readable in games of any language. The available sentences for Like Mail are: The player writes the message when giving this item to be held by a Pokémon. The message can be sent to other games of the same generation by trading the Pokémon holding this item. The message remains available as long as this item is held by a Pokémon or stored in the Mailbox. This item is unable to have any message when stored in the Bag or in the Item Storage System, but there is the option to delete any existing message when storing this item in these places. Once the message is deleted from this item, the player is able to reuse it with a new message. A Pokémon holding this item cannot be placed in the PC or in the Pokémon Day Care. As with other held items, a Pokémon holding this item cannot be transferred into Generation VI games through the Poké Transporter and the Pokémon Bank. This item can be removed by Knock Off. However, it cannot be stolen by Covet, Thief, or the Pickpocket Ability, it causes Bestow and Fling to fail when used by the holder, and causes Switcheroo or Trick to fail when used by or on the holder.

Title: Reply Mail

The Reply Mail (Japanese: おかえしメール Return Mail) is a type of Mail exclusive to the Generation V games. It contains a message with up to three sentence slots which can be filled with words and phrases using the predefined list from the easy chat system. This was designed to be readable in games of any language. The available sentences for Reply Mail are: The player writes the message when giving this item to be held by a Pokémon. The message can be sent to other games of the same generation by trading the Pokémon holding this item. The message remains available as long as this item is held by a Pokémon or stored in the Mailbox. This item is unable to have any message when stored in the Bag or in the Item Storage System, but there is the option to delete any existing message when storing this item in these places. Once the message is deleted from this item, the player is able to reuse it with a new message. A Pokémon holding this item cannot be placed in the PC or in the Pokémon Day Care. As with other held items, a Pokémon holding this item cannot be transferred into Generation VI games through the Poké Transporter and the Pokémon Bank. This item can be removed by Knock Off. However, it cannot be stolen by Covet, Thief, or the Pickpocket Ability, it causes Bestow and Fling to fail when used by the holder, and causes Switcheroo or Trick to fail when used by or on the holder. The Reply Mail is one of the 6 types of Mail featured in a Pokémon Center-exclusive letter set released in November 2021.

Title: Prism Scale

The Prism Scale (Japanese: きれいなウロコ Beautiful Scale) is a type of held item introduced in Generation V. It allows Feebas to evolve into Milotic. Causes Feebas to evolve into Milotic when traded while holding it. This consumes the Prism Scale. This item was introduced in Generation V due to the lack of a means to increase Beauty in these games. However, even in games in which the Prism Scale exists, a Feebas can still evolve if leveled up while its Beauty is high enough. A Prism Scale appears during Chapter 4 of Detective Pikachu, being worn by Milo Green on his headband. He mentions that he obtained the scale when he was once saved from drowning by a Pokémon, which is later revealed to have been a Milotic. The presence of this Prism Scale also acts as the trigger to the Evolution of a Feebas that Milo has befriended. A Prism Scale appeared in The Cuteness Quotient!, where it was attached to Jinny's Feebas. During an attack by Team Rocket, the Prism Scale fell off, but Jinny reattached it to Feebas, causing it to evolve into Milotic. The Prism Scale is the held item featured alongside Feebas in the Held Item Pins line by The Pokémon Company International. In Pokémon Black and White, the Prism Scale was incorrectly categorized in the Bag as a general item rather than a held item. This was corrected in Black 2 and White 2, but was undone in X and Y, causing it to be sorted with valuable items rather than Evolution items in Generations VI and VII.

Title: Eviolite

The Eviolite (Japanese: しんかのきせき Evolution Stone) is a type of held item introduced in Generation V. It boosts the Defense and Special Defense of a Pokémon that holds it if the holder is not fully evolved. If held by a Pokémon that is not fully evolved, its Defense and Special Defense are raised by 50%. The Eviolite only affects Pokémon if their form and species has non-zero value in their "personal data", (internal data that determines the basic traits of any given Pokémon), for an Evolution method, regardless of whether that Evolution method is legitimately accessible. While this functions as expected for most species/forms, there are some notable unusual cases such as instances where an Evolution method "exists" but is inaccessible, as well as some hardcoded exclusions: If only the regional form of a species can evolve, only the regional form is affected by the Eviolite. Meltan is not affected by the Eviolite, because it cannot evolve in the core series games, only being able to evolve in Pokémon GO. It does not affect Cosplay Pikachu, Pikachu in a cap, or Eternal Flower Floette, as those specific forms have no Evolution method coded, even though the base species is capable of evolving. It does not affect Pikachu, Meowth, or Eevee while they have the Gigantamax Factor, as they are hardcoded not to evolve unless the Gigantamax Factor is removed via Max Soup, as well as being hardcoded to not receive the Eviolite boost if they have the Gigantamax Factor. It does affect male Combee and Salandit, despite only female Combee and Salandit being capable of Evolution, as they are the same species and form as their female counterparts and therefore share the "non-zero Evolution value" in their personal data. It does affect certain Pokémon that are capable of Evolution despite it being impossible to meet the requirements for their Evolution. In Pokémon Sword and Shield, Galarian Slowpoke was affected by the Eviolite before the Galarica Cuff or Galarica Wreath were made available in the Pokémon Sword and Shield Expansion Pass. In Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, it affects Ursaring and Stantler despite their Evolution methods being inaccessible within Scarlet and Violet. Hisuian Qwilfish was also similarly affected for the same reason before The Indigo Disk added its Evolution method into Overqwil. Prior to Hydrapple's introduction in The Indigo Disk, Dipplin was affected by the Eviolite. If held by a transformed Pokémon, the boost is based on its original species, not the species it is transformed into. In Challenge the Battle Subway!, an Eviolite was held by Monta's Boldore. Eviolite was introduced as a Pokémon Tool card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game during the English Black & White Series (the Japanese BW Era) in the Noble Victories expansion. When this Trainer card is attached to a Basic Pokémon, any damage done to this Pokémon by attacks is reduced by 20, after applying Weakness and Resistance. Eviolite's name may be derived from Evolution and the -ite suffix found in the names of many minerals. Eviolite's Japanese name is a pun as しんかのきせき (Shinka no Kiseki) can mean either "Evolution Stone" or "Evolution Miracle."

Title: Float Stone

The Float Stone (Japanese: かるいし Pumice Stone) is a type of held item introduced in Generation V. In the Generation V games, the Float Stone can be sold to an item maniac inside the Icirrus City Pokémon Center for $1000. Autotomize is applied before other weight-modifying effects, including the Float Stone. A Pokémon's weight cannot be reduced below 0.1 kg. This has no effect on the Heavy Ball, which uses the Pokémon's species' weight rather than its current weight. In Pokémon UNITE, the Float Stone is a held item that increases the holders movement speed while out of combat and increases their Attack and Speed. 5 seconds after leaving combat, the user's movement speed is increased by 10/15/20% at respectively Grades 1, 10, and 20. Attack and Speed are increased by 1.6 and 0 at Grade 1 and 24 and 150 at Grade 30. Float Stone was introduced as a Pokémon Tool card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game during the English Black & White Series (the Japanese BW Era) in the Plasma Freeze expansion, with artwork by 5ban Graphics. It was reprinted with artwork by Toyste Beach during the XY Series in the BREAKthrough expansion. This Trainer card causes the holder to have no Retreat Cost.

Title: Rocky Helmet

The Rocky Helmet (Japanese: ゴツゴツメット Rugged Helmet) is a type of held item introduced in Generation V. It damages Pokémon that hit the holder with contact moves. If a Pokémon uses a move that makes contact with a Pokémon holding a Rocky Helmet, the Pokémon that used the move takes damage equal to 1/6 of its maximum HP. Each individual strike from a multistrike move that makes contact will activate the Rocky Helmet. If a Pokémon using Thief, Covet, or Knock Off or that has the Ability Magician faints due to the target's held Rocky Helmet, it cannot steal or remove the target's Rocky Helmet. In link battles and battle facilities, if two Trainers' Pokémon faint at the same time due to a held Rocky Helmet, the Trainer whose Pokémon held the Rocky Helmet wins. Due to a bug, in version 1.0 of Pokémon Sun and Moon, in link battles and battle facilities, if two Trainers' Pokémon faint at the same time due to a held Rocky Helmet, the Trainer whose Pokémon held the Rocky Helmet wins. From version 1.1 of Pokémon Sun and Moon onward, the Trainer whose Pokémon did not hold the Rocky Helmet wins. In Pokémon UNITE, the Rocky Helmet is a held item that causes recoil to nearby enemies after taking damage equal or greater than 10% max HP in one strike (with a cooldown of 2s). This damage is equal to 3/4/5% of user's max HP at grades 1, 10, and 20 respectively. This item also increases user's HP and Defense: 18 and 0 respectively at Grade 1, and 270 and 51 at Grade 30. In Under the Pledging Tree!, a Rocky Helmet was one of the items offered to Ash by a market vendor in Coumarine City after Ash had wondered out loud what presents he should buy for an upcoming festival. Rocky Helmet was introduced as a Pokémon Tool card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game during the English Black & White Series (the Japanese BW Era) in the Noble Victories expansion (the Japanese Red Collection), with artwork by Ryo Ueda. It was reprinted as both a Regular and an Ultra Rare Secret card in the English Boundaries Crossed expansion with artwork by the same illustrator. During the English Sword & Shield Series (the Japanese SWSH Era) the card was reprinted in the Vivid Voltage expansion (the Japanese Legendary Heartbeat subset) with artwork by Studio Bora Inc. For the Scarlet & Violet Series (Era in Japan), Rocky Helmet was reprinted again with artwork by Ayaka Yoshida. This print features the new Trainer card layout and Pokémon Tool card category introduced at the start of this Series. When this card is attached to the player's Active Pokémon, if an opponent deals damage to that Pokémon (even if it is Knocked Out), the player can put two damage counters on the Attacking Pokémon. A strap featuring Scraggy and a Rocky Helmet has been released on January 19, 2013 as part of the Pokémon Goods For Adventure Campaign. It was exclusive to the Pokémon Center. The Rocky Helmet has also been featured on a mini towel that was released on March 27, 2025 as part of the Pokémon Sento goods collection exclusive to the Pokémon Center.

Title: Air Balloon

Balloon redirects here. For Team Rocket's balloon, see Team Rocket's mechas#Meowth Balloon. The Air Balloon (Japanese: ふうせん Balloon) is a type of held item introduced in Generation V. It makes the holder ungrounded. The Air Balloon makes the holder ungrounded, which means that the holder is immune to damaging Ground-type moves and several related effects. If the holder or its substitute is hit by a damaging move (even if it has Disguise), the Air Balloon is destroyed. A message is displayed when a Pokémon is sent into battle while holding an Air Balloon (if its effect is not negated). The Air Balloon does not make the holder ungrounded while Gravity is in effect or if it is under the effect of Ingrain, Smack Down, or Thousand Arrows (but this does not prevent it from bursting). An Air Balloon will not burst while Magic Room is in effect, if the holder has the Ability Klutz or is under the effect of Embargo. A burst Air Balloon cannot be recovered with Recycle or Pickup. The Air Balloon bursts before it can be stolen by Thief, Covet, or Magician. Whether a Pokémon is affected by terrain is determined before the Air Balloon bursts, so terrain cannot prevent a Pokémon holding an Air Balloon from being afflicted by a status condition from the move that bursts the Air Balloon. An Air Balloon bursting will trigger Unburden and Symbiosis. An Air Balloon appeared in Challenge the Battle Subway!, being held by Monta's Snivy. The Air Balloon was introduced as a Pokémon Tool card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game during the English Sword & Shield Series (the Japanese Sword & Shield Era). It was first released in the Japanese Sword expansion before debuting in English in the Sword & Shield expansion, with artwork by Yoshinobu Saito. It was also released as a Secret Full Art card in the same Japanese and English expansion, with artwork by the same artist. It reduces the Retreat Cost of the holder by (2 Energy).

Title: Red Card

The Red Card (Japanese: レッドカード Red Card) is a type of consumable held item introduced in Generation V. If the holder is hit by another Pokémon's damaging move, it switches out the attacker. If the holder is hit by a damaging move and does not faint, it consumes the Red Card and forces the Pokémon that used the move (even if it was an ally) to switch to a random Pokémon. If held by a wild Pokémon in a Single Battle, it forces a player's Pokémon that attacks it to flee when activated. In a wild battle that was initiated as a Horde Encounter or Double Battle, or an SOS Battle in which there are currently two wild Pokémon, the Red Card will cause the player's attacking Pokémon to switch rather than flee. If multiple Pokémon, each holding a Red Card, are hit by a single attack, only the fastest holder's held Red Card will activate. The Red Card will not activate if the attacker is a wild Pokémon or its Trainer has no Pokémon to switch in, even if the Red Card would cause the attacker to flee rather than switch out. The Red Card will not activate if a Pokémon's substitute takes the hit instead. If a move is boosted by the effect of Sheer Force, that move will not activate the Red Card. The Red Card activates after the last strike of a multistrike move. If the attacker has Suction Cups, is a Dondozo with a Tatsugiri in its mouth because of Commander, or is under the effect of Ingrain, the Red Card will still activate and be consumed, but fails to cause that Pokémon to switch out or flee (even if that attacker is Ghost-type, has the Ability Run Away, or is holding a Shed Shell or Smoke Ball). Trapping moves (besides Ingrain) and trapping Abilities do not prevent a Pokémon from being forced to switch out or flee by the Red Card. The Red Card will be knocked off or stolen by Knock Off, Thief, Covet, or Magician before it has the opportunity to activate. A Pokémon with Pickpocket holding a Red Card can steal an attacker's item with Pickpocket even after that attacker is switched out by the Pokémon's held Red Card; if a Pokémon steals a Red Card due to Pickpocket, that Red Card cannot activate for that same attack. The Red Card switches out the attacker before its own held Life Orb or Shell Bell has a chance to activate. If a Pokémon holding a Red Card is hit by a combination move (Grass Pledge, Fire Pledge, or Water Pledge) without fainting, and the attacker is switched out, the combination effect will not be created. If a Pokémon holding a Red Card is hit by Dragon Tail or Circle Throw without fainting, it is forced to switch out or flee without giving its Red Card the opportunity to activate. If a Pokémon holding a Red Card is hit by U-turn, Volt Switch or Flip Turn without fainting, the Red Card activates and forces the attacker to switch to a random Pokémon, instead of a Pokémon of their Trainer's choice. Emergency Exit and Wimp Out will not activate if a Red Card is used up on the same move, while U-turn, Volt Switch and Flip Turn will not cause the user to switch if they are holding a Red Card. Dynamaxed Pokémon cannot be switched out by the Red Card, but the item will still be consumed. In Pokémon Trading Card Game Pocket, Red Card appears as an Item card that forces a player's opponent to shuffle their hand into their deck and draw three cards. Red Card was introduced as an Item card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game during the English XY Series (the Japanese XY Era) in the XY expansion. This Trainer card makes the opponent shuffle their hand into their deck and draw 4 cards.

Title: Ring Target

The Ring Target (Japanese: ねらいのまと Aim Target) is a type of held item introduced in Generation V. It removes the holder's type immunities to moves. The holder loses its type immunities to attacking moves. It does not affect other type-based immunities, such as Grass-type Pokémon being immune to powder and spore moves, but does make Ground-type Pokémon vulnerable to Thunder Wave. Flying-type Pokémon become vulnerable to Ground-type moves, but are otherwise considered ungrounded.

Title: Binding Band

The Binding Band (Japanese: しめつけバンド Binding Band) is a type of held item introduced in Generation V. Causes binding moves used by the holder to inflict binding that deals damage equal to 1/8 (rather than 1/16) of the target's maximum HP at the end of each turn. Causes binding moves used by the holder to inflict binding that deals damage equal to 1/6 (rather than 1/8) of the target's maximum HP at the end of each turn. Prior to Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, the Binding Band is a Möbius strip. In its Scarlet and Violet artwork, it is instead depicted as a regular two-sided band.

Title: Absorb Bulb

The Absorb Bulb (Japanese: きゅうこん Bulb) is a type of held item introduced in Generation V. It raises the holder's Special Attack when it is hit by a damaging Water-type move. If the holder is hit by a Water-type damaging move, it consumes the Absorb Bulb and its Special Attack is increased by one stage. It does not activate if the holder already has +6 Special Attack stat stages (unless its Ability is Contrary, in which case it will not activate if the holder has -6 Special Attack stat stages). It does not activate if the holder is protected from or unaffected by the Water-type move (e.g. due to Water Absorb or Wonder Guard). If multiple Pokémon each holding an Absorb Bulb or Luminous Moss are hit by a Water-type move at once, each hit Pokémon will consume their Absorb Bulb or Luminous Moss before Symbiosis transfers any item (including these items).

Title: Cell Battery

The Cell Battery (Japanese: じゅうでんち Charge Battery) is a type of held item introduced in Generation V. It raises the holder's Attack when it is hit by a damaging Electric-type move. If the holder is hit by an Electric-type damaging move, it consumes the Cell Battery and its Attack is increased by one stage. It does not activate if the holder already has +6 Attack stat stages (unless its Ability is Contrary, in which case it will not activate if the holder has -6 Attack stat stages). It does not activate if the holder is protected from or unaffected by the Electric-type move (e.g. due to Motor Drive or being Ground-type). If multiple Pokémon each holding a Cell Battery are hit by an Electric-type move at once, each hit Pokémon will consume its Cell Battery before Symbiosis transfers any item (including Cell Batteries).

Title: Eject Button

The Eject Button (Japanese: だっしゅつボタン Break-Out Button) is a type of consumable held item introduced in Generation V. It switches out the holder if it is hit by a damaging move. If the holder is hit by a damaging move and does not faint, it consumes the Eject Button and is immediately switched out for a Pokémon of its Trainer's choice. It does not activate if the holder is a wild Pokémon or its Trainer has no Pokémon to switch in. The Eject Button will not activate if a Pokémon's substitute takes the hit instead. If a move is affected by the effect of Sheer Force, that move will not activate the Eject Button. The Eject Button activates after the last strike of a multistrike move. If multiple Pokémon, each holding an Eject Button, are hit by a single attack, only the fastest holder's held Eject Button will activate. Suction Cups, trapping moves, Abilities, and Dynamax do not prevent a Pokémon being switched out by the Eject Button. The Eject Button will be removed from the holder by Knock Off, Thief, Covet, or Magician before it has the opportunity to activate. If a Pokémon steals an Eject Button due to Pickpocket, that Eject Button cannot activate for that same attack. If a Pokémon holding an Eject Button is hit by Dragon Tail or Circle Throw, it is forced to switch out or flee before its held Eject Button has the opportunity to activate. If U-turn or Volt Switch causes a Pokémon's held Eject Button to activate, the Pokémon that used the move is not switched out. If a Pokémon with Emergency Exit or Wimp Out holds an Eject Button, the Eject Button activates before the Ability has the opportunity to. The Eject Button is involved in the Symbiosis Eject Button glitch. If an ally's Eject Button is consumed, Symbiosis will activate and transfer an item to that ally after it switches out but before its replacement is sent out. Any item transferred in response to an Eject Button activating cannot activate at this time. The Eject Button activating can no longer trigger Symbiosis. In Pokémon UNITE, the Eject Button is a battle item unlocked at Trainer Level 11. It can be used to teleport to a set distance away and has 70s cooldown.

Title: Fire Gem

The Fire Gem (Japanese: ほのおのジュエル Flame Jewel) is a type of held item introduced in Generation V. It is one of the Gems and is an unobtainable held item in Generation VI onwards. When held by a Pokémon, it increases the power of the first Fire-type move used by 50%, and is consumed after use. Fling will fail if the user is holding a Fire Gem. Fire Pledge will never consume a Fire Gem. When held by a Pokémon, it increases the power of the first Fire-type move used by 30%, and is consumed after use. A Fire Gem appeared in Climbing the Tower of Success! as one of the search items for the Wishing Bell Festival scavenger hunt.

Title: Water Gem

The Water Gem (Japanese: みずのジュエル Water Jewel) is a type of held item introduced in Generation V. It is one of the Gems and is an unobtainable held item in Generation VI onwards. When held by a Pokémon, it increases the power of the first Water-type move used by 50%, and is consumed after use. Fling will fail if the user is holding a Water Gem. Water Pledge will never consume a Water Gem. When held by a Pokémon, it increases the power of the first Water-type move used by 30%, and is consumed after use.

Title: Electric Gem

The Electric Gem (Japanese: でんきのジュエル Electric Jewel) is a type of held item introduced in Generation V. It is one of the Gems and is an unobtainable held item in Generation VI onwards. When held by a Pokémon, it increases the power of the first Electric-type move used by 50%, and is consumed after use. Fling will fail if the user is holding an Electric Gem. When held by a Pokémon, it increases the power of the first Electric-type move used by 30%, and is consumed after use.

Title: Grass Gem

When held by a Pokémon, it increases the power of the first Grass-type move used by 50%, and is consumed after use. Fling will fail if the user is holding a Grass Gem. Grass Pledge will never consume a Grass Gem. When held by a Pokémon, it increases the power of the first Grass-type move used by 30%, and is consumed after use.

Title: Ice Gem

The Ice Gem (Japanese: こおりのジュエル Ice Jewel) is a type of held item introduced in Generation V. It is one of the Gems and is an unobtainable held item in Generation VI onwards. When held by a Pokémon, it increases the power of the first Ice-type move used by 50%, and is consumed after use. Fling will fail if the user is holding an Ice Gem. When held by a Pokémon, it increases the power of the first Ice-type move used by 30%, and is consumed after use.

Title: Fighting Gem

When held by a Pokémon, it increases the power of the first Fighting-type move used by 50%, and is consumed after use. Fling will fail if the user is holding a Fighting Gem. When held by a Pokémon, it increases the power of the first Fighting-type move used by 30%, and is consumed after use.

Title: Poison Gem

The Poison Gem (Japanese: どくのジュエル Poison Jewel) is a type of held item introduced in Generation V. It is one of the Gems and is an unobtainable held item in Generation VI onwards. When held by a Pokémon, it increases the power of the first Poison-type move used by 50%, and is consumed after use. Fling will fail if the user is holding a Poison Gem. When held by a Pokémon, it increases the power of the first Poison-type move used by 30%, and is consumed after use.

Title: Ground Gem

The Ground Gem (Japanese: じめんのジュエル Ground Jewel) is a type of held item introduced in Generation V. It is one of the Gems and is an unobtainable held item in Generation VI onwards. When held by a Pokémon, it increases the power of the first Ground-type move used by 50%, and is consumed after use. Fling will fail if the user is holding a Ground Gem. When held by a Pokémon, it increases the power of the first Ground-type move used by 30%, and is consumed after use.

Title: Flying Gem

The Flying Gem (Japanese: ひこうのジュエル Flying Jewel) is a type of held item introduced in Generation V. It is one of the Gems and is an unobtainable held item in Generation VI onwards. When held by a Pokémon, it increases the power of the first Flying-type move used by 50%, and is consumed after use. Fling will fail if the user is holding a Flying Gem. Acrobatics will be counted as the user not holding an item and thus receive double base power (in addition to the power increase from the Gem) if the user consumes a Flying Gem before executing it. When held by a Pokémon, it increases the power of the first Flying-type move used by 30%, and is consumed after use.

Title: Dark Gem

The Dark Gem (Japanese: あくのジュエル Evil Jewel) is a type of held item introduced in Generation V. It is one of the Gems and is an unobtainable held item in Generation VI onwards. When held by a Pokémon, it increases the power of the first Dark-type move used by 50%, and is consumed after use. Fling will fail if the user is holding a Dark Gem. When held by a Pokémon, it increases the power of the first Dark-type move used by 30%, and is consumed after use.

Title: Steel Gem

The Steel Gem (Japanese: はがねのジュエル Steel Jewel) is a type of held item introduced in Generation V. It is one of the Gems and is an unobtainable held item in Generation VI onwards. When held by a Pokémon, it increases the power of the first Steel-type move used by 50%, and is consumed after use. Fling will fail if the user is holding a Steel Gem. When held by a Pokémon, it increases the power of the first Steel-type move used by 30%, and is consumed after use.

Title: Cover Fossil

The Cover Fossil (Japanese: ふたのカセキ Cover Fossil) is a fossil introduced in Generation V that can be regenerated into Tirtouga. It is the counterpart of the Plume Fossil. If brought to the one of the following locations, the Cover Fossil can be regenerated into a Tirtouga. A Cover Fossil appeared in A Restoration Confrontation! Part 1. It was found by Ferris and later stolen by Team Rocket, who—by using the stolen data from Professor Juniper and Fennel's fossil resurrection machine and the stones stolen from the Chargestone Cave—managed to revive it into a Tirtouga. It evolved into a Carracosta in the next episode and was eventually returned back to its own time through a time gate at Twist Mountain. The Cover Fossil was introduced as an Item card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game in the Noble Victories expansion, first released in the Japanese Red Collection. It allows players to look at the bottom seven cards of their deck, reveal a Tirtouga they find there, and put it onto their Bench. The other six cards are then shuffled back into the deck.cards are then shuffled back into the deck. Main article: Antique Cover Fossil (Stellar Crown 129) The Scarlet & Violet Series saw the release of Antique Cover Fossil in the Stellar Crown expansion, first released in the Japanese Stellar Miracle expansion. This card is unlike the previous Cover Fossil card and more in line with other Item cards depicting Fossils, allowing the player to play it onto the Bench as if it were a Colorless-type Basic Pokémon. It has an Ability that prevents all effects of attacks used by the opponent's Pokémon done to it. Unidentified Fossil cards are Fossil Trainer cards which become Basic Pokémon. While they do not feature a Cover Fossil, they can still be used to evolve into certain Tirtouga. The Cover Fossil has been featured in several officially licensed merchandise.

Title: Plume Fossil

The Plume Fossil (Japanese: はねのカセキ Feather Fossil) is a fossil introduced in Generation V that can be regenerated into Archen. It is the counterpart of the Cover Fossil. If brought to the one of the following locations, the Plume Fossil can be regenerated into an Archen. A Plume Fossil appeared in Archeops In The Modern World!, which had been given to Professor Juniper and Fennel by Lenora. They successfully resurrected a female Archen from the Fossil using Musharna's dream energy. With the help of Ash and his Tranquill, Archen learned how to fly and then evolved into Archeops. Kiawe found a Plume Fossil in Treasure Hunt, Akala Style!, during the Stoutland Search treasure hunt set up for Ash and his classmates by Olivia. A Plume Fossil also appeared on a holographic screen during Sophocles's first hunt for treasure. The Plume Fossil was introduced as an Item card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game in the Noble Victories expansion, first released in the Japanese Red Collection. It allows players to look at the bottom seven cards of their deck, reveal an Archen they find there, and put it onto their Bench. The other six cards are then shuffled back into the deck. Main article: Antique Plume Fossil (White Flare 79) The Scarlet & Violet Series saw the release of Antique Plume Fossil in the White Flare expansion, first released in the Japanese expansion with the same name. This card is unlike the previous Plume Fossil card and more in line with other Item cards depicting Fossils, allowing the player to play it onto the Bench as if it were a Colorless-type Basic Pokémon. It has an Ability that prevents all damage done to it by attacks used by the opponent's Pokémon if it is on the Bench. Unidentified Fossil cards are Fossil Trainer cards which become Basic Pokémon. While they do not feature a Plume Fossil, they can still be used to evolve into certain Archen. The Plume Fossil has been featured in several officially licensed merchandise. While the item description for the Plume Fossil states the fossil came from a "Pokémon that (once) flew/lived in the sky", the Backpacker who gives away the fossils in Pokémon Black and White mentions that the Plume Fossil is from a Pokémon that lived in an ancient forest, but could not fly. Considering what is known about the Archaeopteryx genus, this may be intentional to some degree.

Title: Liberty Pass

The Liberty Pass (Japanese: リバティチケット Liberty Ticket) is an event-exclusive Key Item that allows a player to travel to Liberty Garden where they will meet Victini in Pokémon Black and White. Once the Liberty Pass is obtained, the player must take it to Liberty Pier, the westernmost pier of the Castelia City harbor. The captain of the boat docked here will recognize the Liberty Pass, and volunteer to take the player to Liberty Garden. If the player does not have the Liberty Pass, the vessel remains stationary. The Liberty Pass was distributed wirelessly at the release of Black and White in all geographic regions and in some retail outlets outside of Japan. The release length differs between countries. A pair of Liberty Passes appeared in At Liberty on Liberty Garden under the ownership of White. She and Black used them to board a ship from Castelia City to Liberty Garden in order to search for Victini per Professor Juniper's request.

Title: Pass Orb

A Pass Orb (Japanese: デルダマ Emerging Sphere) is a type of item introduced and used exclusively in Generation V. Pass Orbs can be used to purchase Pass Powers at the Entralink.

Title: Dream Ball

The Dream Ball (Japanese: ドリームボール Dream Ball) is a type of Poké Ball introduced in Generation V. It can be used to catch a wild Pokémon in the Entree Forest. Starting from Generation VIII, it is more likely to succeed when used on a sleeping Pokémon. Pokémon transferred from the Pokémon Dream Radar are inside Dream Balls. In the Entree Forest, whenever the player interacts with a Pokémon, a special wild encounter begins in which the player can catch the Pokémon without battling it. During this encounter, a single Dream Ball appears in the player's Bag, in addition to their regular Poké Balls; if the player does not use the Dream Ball, it vanishes after the encounter. Any Poké Ball used in the Entree Forest will catch the wild Pokémon without fail. When used from the Bag in a wild encounter, it attempts to catch the wild Pokémon. If used on a sleeping Pokémon, it has a 4× catch rate modifier; otherwise, it has a 1× modifier. In Pokémon Legends: Z-A, if used on a drowsy Pokémon, it has a 4× catch rate modifier. Although some Pokémon can sleep in the overworld as part of their natural behavior, this will not trigger the Dream Ball's bonus. Despite being unobtainable as an item in Generation VI, a Dream Ball appears in the Poké Ball Boutique in Lumiose City. Dream Ball was introduced as an Item card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game during the English Sword & Shield Series (the Japanese Sword & Shield Era). It was first released in the Japanese Eevee Heroes subset before debuting in English in the Evolving Skies expansion, with artwork by Ryo Ueda. It allows the player to search their deck for a Pokémon and put it onto their Bench, but only if the Dream Ball was taken as a face-down Prize card. Notably, this includes Evolution Pokémon that normally can only enter play through Evolution. The design of the Dream Ball is similar to the design of Musharna.

Title: Prop Case

The Prop Case (Japanese: グッズケース Goods Case) is a Key Item introduced in Generation V. The Prop Case stores the Props used to dress up Pokémon for Pokémon Musicals. In Pokémon Black 2 and White 2, if the player Memory Linked with Black or White, the Prop Case will come prepackaged with the Props obtained in that linked game. The Prop Case, along with the other elements and concept of the Pokémon Musical, was invented by White, and was first introduced in To Make a Musical. In Special Delivery, the truck delivering the 200 Prop Cases meant to be distributed during the Pokémon Musical opening got stuck in traffic behind the lifted Driftveil Drawbridge. As such, White asked Skyla to help, and with her plane, the Prop Cases made it to Nimbasa City in time.

Title: Dragon Skull

The Dragon Skull (Japanese: ドラゴンのホネ Dragon Bone) is a Key Item introduced in Generation V. It is the skull of a Dragon-type Pokémon that belongs to the Nacrene Museum, and seems to be from a Dragonite. Team Plasma steals this Dragon Skull from Nacrene Museum after the player earns their Basic Badge. The player confronts the culprit, a Team Plasma Grunt at the far end of Pinwheel Forest, and retrieves the skull, and immediately returns it to Lenora. Because the player obtains and gives away the skull in the same cutscene, they are not able to view it in their Bag. In Pokémon Adventures, instead of merely the skull, Team Plasma stole the entire Dragon Fossil in The Mystery of the Missing Fossil, along with several other artifacts from the Nacrene Museum. Black agreed to help find the thieves, and, during his pursuit of the stolen items, encountered Burgh, whom Lenora had also requested to help solve the case. The two soon found the Dragon Fossil at the outskirts of Pinwheel Forest, where it, to their surprise, then began attacking them. Black soon figured out that the ones responsible for the theft were a group of Team Plasma Grunts and Gorm of the Seven Sages, and they were using Gorm's Cofagrigus to control the skeleton. With Burgh's help, Black was able to defeat Cofagrigus. Upon realizing that the skeleton wasn't related to the Legendary Pokémon they were after, Team Plasma retreated, allowing the Dragon Fossil and the other stolen artifacts to be safely returned to the Nacrene Museum. In Pokémon X and Y, a Dragon Skull can be seen on a shelf in the Ambrette Town Fossil Lab.

Title: Balm Mushroom

The Balm Mushroom (Japanese: かおるキノコ Fragrant Mushroom), formatted BalmMushroom prior to Generation VI, is a valuable item introduced in Generation V. In the Generation V games, the BalmMushroom can be sold to the gourmet maniac on Route 5 for $25000. In Pokémon Sword and Shield, the Balm Mushroom can be sold to the valuable item buyer in Stow-on-Side for $25,000. The Balm Mushroom serves no practical purpose other than to be sold. A Balm Mushroom is an ingredient that can be used in cooking to attract wild Pokémon in Pokémon Quest. It is categorized as a Mushroom. Balm Mushroom is a possible ingredient that can be obtained by completing expeditions. Balm Mushrooms are most commonly found in the areas Parched Peak and Farside Fjord. They can also be obtained randomly by recycling Power Stones. In Food Fit for a Kingambit!, Balm Mushrooms were served as part of the Anything Goes Café's House Specials.

Title: Big Nugget

The Big Nugget (Japanese: でかいきんのたま Giant Gold Orb) is a valuable item introduced in Generation V. It is a larger and more valuable version of the Nugget. In the Generation V games, the Big Nugget can be sold to the ore maniac inside the Icirrus City Pokémon Center for $30000. In Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon, the player can purchase a Big Nugget from Office Worker Royce for $5,000 in Po Town daily. The Big Nugget serves no practical purpose other than to be sold. Similarly to the Nugget's Japanese name きんのたま kin no tama ("golden ball") being a slang term for testicles in Japanese; the Big Nugget's Japanese name adds でかい dekai; literally making it a gigantic testicle. Starting from Pokémon Sword and Shield, the Big Nugget is tied with the Iron Ball for granting Fling its highest possible base power.

Title: Pearl String

The Pearl String (Japanese: おだんごしんじゅ Pearl Dango) is a valuable item introduced in Generation V. In the Generation V games, the Pearl String can be sold to the ore maniac inside the Icirrus City Pokémon Center for $25000. In Pokémon Sword and Shield, the Pearl String can be sold to the valuable item buyer in Stow-on-Side for $27,500. The Pearl String serves no practical purpose other than to be sold. The Pearl String has been featured on an illustration for the Pokémon Diving merchandise line that was exclusive to the Pokémon Center.

Title: Comet Shard

The Comet Shard (Japanese: すいせいのかけら Comet Shard) is a valuable item introduced in Generation V. In the Generation V games, the Comet Shard can be sold to the ore maniac inside the Icirrus City Pokémon Center for $60000. In Pokémon Sword and Shield, the Comet Shard can be sold to the valuable item buyer in Stow-on-Side for $30,000. The Comet Shard serves no practical purpose other than to be sold. In the dub of Treasure Hunt, Akala Style!, the Star Piece that Sophocles found during a Stoutland Search treasure hunt was incorrectly referred to as a Comet Shard.

Title: Relic Copper

The Relic Copper (Japanese: こだいのどうか Ancient Bronze Coin) is a valuable item introduced in Generation V. In the Generation V games, the Relic Copper can be sold to the billionaire maniac in Undella Town for $1000. The Relic Copper serves no practical purpose other than to be sold.

Title: Relic Silver

The Relic Silver (Japanese: こだいのぎんか Ancient Silver Coin) is a valuable item introduced in Generation V. In the Generation V games, the Relic Silver can be sold to the billionaire maniac in Undella Town for $5000. The Relic Silver serves no practical purpose other than to be sold.

Title: Relic Gold

The Relic Gold (Japanese: こだいのきんか Ancient Gold Coin) is a valuable item introduced in Generation V. In the Generation V games, the Relic Gold can be sold to the billionaire maniac in Undella Town for $10000. The Relic Gold serves no practical purpose other than to be sold.

Title: Relic Vase

The Relic Vase (Japanese: こだいのツボ Ancient Vase) is a valuable item introduced in Generation V. In the Generation V games, the Relic Vase can be sold to the billionaire maniac in Undella Town for $50000. The Relic Vase serves no practical purpose other than to be sold.

Title: Relic Band

The Relic Band (Japanese: こだいのうでわ Ancient Bracelet) is a valuable item introduced in Generation V. In the Generation V games, the Relic Band can be sold to the billionaire maniac in Undella Town for $100000. The Relic Band serves no practical purpose other than to be sold.
