Title: Rainbow Flower

The Rainbow Flower (Japanese: にじいろのはな Rainbow Flower) is a Key Item introduced in Generation VII. It is one of the items the player collects during Mina's trial in Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon. Starts the final part of Mina's trial, a battle with Totem Ribombee. The Rainbow Flower was a Pokémon Tool card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game exclusively released in Japan. This card was one of seven possible SM-P Promotional cards available in promotional packs awarded to participants of Pokémon Card Gym tournaments during September, October, and November 2019. This print features the Pokémon Card Gym logo. When it's attached to a Pokémon-, that Pokémon gets +20 HP, and can't be damaged by the opponent's attacks while on the Bench.

Title: Surge Badge

The Surge Badge (Japanese: だいだいバッジ Orange Badge) is a Key Item introduced in Generation VII. It is a novelty item that can be obtained in the Kantonian Gym in Malie City by defeating its leader. It appears to be based on the Vermilion Gym's Thunder Badge and named after its Gym Leader.

Title: Roto Hatch

The Roto Hatch (Japanese: タマゴふかポン Roto Hatch) is a Rotom Power introduced in Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon. When used in the overworld, the number of cycles it takes for all Pokémon Eggs in the party to hatch are halved. This effect stacks with Flame Body or Magma Armor.

Title: Roto Bargain

The Roto Bargain (Japanese: やすうりポン Roto Bargain) is a Rotom Power introduced in Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon. When used in the overworld, prices of products at Poké Marts are reduced by 50%.

Title: Roto Prize Money

The Roto Prize Money (Japanese: おこづかいポン Roto Prize Money) is a Rotom Power introduced in Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon. When used in the overworld, the amount of prize money received after battles is tripled.

Title: Roto Friendship

The Roto Friendship (Japanese: なつきポン Roto Friendship) is a Rotom Power introduced in Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon.

Title: Roto Encounter

The Roto Encounter (Japanese: みっけポン Roto Encounter) is a Rotom Power introduced in Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon.

Title: Roto Stealth

The Roto Stealth (Japanese: かくれポン Roto Stealth) is a Rotom Power introduced in Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon.

Title: Roto Boost

The Roto Boost (Japanese: おうえんポン Roto Boost) is a Rotom Power introduced in Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon. When used from the Bag during battle, it raises the Attack, Defense, Special Attack, Special Defense and Speed of the Pokémon currently in battle by one stage each.

Title: Roto Catch

The Roto Catch (Japanese: つかまえポン Roto Catch) is a Rotom Power introduced in Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon.

Title: Autograph

The Autograph (Japanese: マチスのサイン Matisse's Signature) is an item introduced in Generation VII. In the Japanese version of this item description, Lt. Surge wrote "Good luck!" in English as well. This is related to his habit of using English words and phrases in the Japanese dialogue. This "Good luck!" text found in the Japanese version is written using regular English letters, not katakana. An identical autograph to this one can also be found in the player's room in Pokémon Ultra Sun and Ultra Moon.

Title: Silver Razz Berry

The Silver Razz Berry (Japanese: ぎんのズリのみ Silver Zuri Fruit) is a type of Berry introduced in Generation VII. It is a more effective counterpart to the Razz Berry for catching Pokémon. When used from the Bag on a wild Pokémon, it increases its catch rate. The effect wears off if and when the wild Pokémon breaks out of a thrown Poké Ball.

Title: Golden Razz Berry

The Golden Razz Berry (Japanese: きんのズリのみ Gold Zuri Fruit) is a type of Berry introduced in Generation VII. It is a variant of the Razz Berry and a more effective counterpart to the Silver Razz Berry for catching Pokémon. When used from the Bag on a wild Pokémon, it drastically increases its catch rate. The effect wears off if and when the wild Pokémon breaks out of a thrown Poké Ball. The Golden Razz Berry was added to Pokémon GO on June 22, 2017. It can be used to make wild Pokémon significantly more likely to be caught, adding a ×2.5 multiplier to the catch rate. The Berry's effect lasts until the Pokémon breaks out of a thrown Poké Ball. If a Golden Razz Berry is fed to a Gym defender, it will completely fill a Pokémon's motivation, regardless of how many Berries it has already eaten. Golden Razz Berries can be repeatably obtained from Spinning a PokéStop identified as interesting by an Ultra Buddy Golden Razz Berries have also been obtainable from certain Special Research tasks.

Title: Silver Nanab Berry

The Silver Nanab Berry (Japanese: ぎんのナナのみ Silver Nana Fruit) is a type of Berry introduced in Generation VII. It is a more effective variant of the Nanab Berry for catching Pokémon. When used from the Bag on a wild Pokémon, it makes the wild Pokémon less likely to move and the target ring shrink slower. The effect wears off if and when the wild Pokémon breaks out of a thrown Poké Ball.

Title: Golden Nanab Berry

The Golden Nanab Berry (Japanese: きんのナナのみ Gold Nana Fruit) is a type of Berry introduced in Generation VII. It is a variant of the Nanab Berry and more effective than the Silver Nanab Berry for catching Pokémon. When used from the Bag on a wild Pokémon, it significantly makes the wild Pokémon less likely to move and the target ring shrink slower. The effect wears off if and when the wild Pokémon breaks out of a thrown Poké Ball.

Title: Silver Pinap Berry

The Silver Pinap Berry (Japanese: ぎんのパイルのみ Silver Pairu Fruit) is a type of Berry introduced in Generation VII. It is a more effective variant of the Pinap Berry for catching Pokémon. When used from the Bag on a wild Pokémon, it increases the chance of getting more item rewards for catching Pokémon. The effect wears off if and when the wild Pokémon breaks out of a thrown Poké Ball. The Silver Pinap Berry adds a ×2.334 multiplier to the Candy reward, more than from a normal Pinap Berry, and adds a ×1.8 multiplier to the catch rate of a wild Pokémon. The Berry's effect lasts until the wild Pokémon breaks out of a thrown Poké Ball. Feeding a Silver Pinap Berry to a Gym defender increases its motivation by twice as much as Razz and Pinap Berries do. Silver Pinap Berries can be repeatably obtained from Spinning a PokéStop identified as interesting by an Ultra Buddy Silver Pinap Berries have also been obtainable from certain Special Research tasks.

Title: Golden Pinap Berry

The Golden Pinap Berry (Japanese: きんのパイルのみ Gold Pairu Fruit) is a type of Berry introduced in Generation VII. It is a variant of the Pinap Berry and more effective than the Silver Pinap Berry for catching Pokémon. When used from the Bag on a wild Pokémon, it significantly increases the chance of getting more Candies and Berries for catching it. The effect wears off if and when the wild Pokémon breaks out of a thrown Poké Ball.

Title: Stretchy Spring

The Stretchy Spring (Japanese: のびたバネ Stretched Spring) is a valuable item introduced in Generation VII. The Stretchy Spring serves no practical purpose other than to be sold. The Stretchy Spring is a Buddy Souvenir and serves no functional purpose. A Stretchy Spring is a possible Souvenir the player's Buddy Pokémon can occasionally collect once their Buddy Level has reached "Ultra Buddy".

Title: Chalky Stone

The Chalky Stone (Japanese: チョークいし Chalkstone) is a valuable item introduced in Generation VII. The Chalky Stone serves no practical purpose other than to be sold. The Chalky Stone is a Buddy Souvenir and serves no functional purpose. A Chalky Stone is a possible Souvenir the player's Buddy Pokémon can occasionally collect once their Buddy Level has reached "Ultra Buddy".

Title: Marble

If you were looking for the character of the day who appeared in Luxray Vision!, see Marble (DP059). If you were looking for the character of the day who appeared in Crisis at Ferroseed Research! with the Japanese name of Professor Marble, see Professor Malveaux. The Marble (Japanese: ビーだま Marble) is a valuable item introduced in Generation VII. The Marble serves no practical purpose other than to be sold. The Marble is a Buddy Souvenir and serves no functional purpose. A Marble is a possible Souvenir the player's Buddy Pokémon can occasionally collect once their Buddy Level has reached "Ultra Buddy".

Title: Lone Earring

The Lone Earring (Japanese: かたっぽピアス Lone Earring) is a valuable item introduced in Generation VII. The Lone Earring serves no practical purpose other than to be sold. The Lone Earring is a Buddy Souvenir and serves no functional purpose. A Lone Earring is a possible Souvenir the player's Buddy Pokémon can occasionally collect once their Buddy Level has reached "Ultra Buddy".

Title: Beach Glass

The Beach Glass (Japanese: うみべのガラス Beach Glass) is a valuable item introduced in Generation VII. The Beach Glass serves no practical purpose other than to be sold. The Beach Glass is a Buddy Souvenir and serves no functional purpose. A Beach Glass is a possible Souvenir the player's Buddy Pokémon can occasionally collect once their Buddy Level has reached "Ultra Buddy".

Title: Gold Leaf

If you were looking for the item in Pokémon HeartGold and SoulSilver, see Shiny Leaf. The Gold Leaf (Japanese: きんのはっぱ Gold Leaf) is a valuable item introduced in Generation II. The Gold Leaf serves no practical purpose other than to be sold.

Title: Silver Leaf

The Silver Leaf (Japanese: ぎんのはっぱ Silver Leaf) is a valuable item introduced in Generation II. The Silver Leaf serves no practical purpose other than to be sold. For Pokémon distributed in Generation I games, the Pokémon must be traded to a Generation II game for the item to appear.

Title: Polished Mud Ball

The Polished Mud Ball (Japanese: ピカピカだんご Shining Mud Ball) is a valuable item introduced in Generation VII. The Polished Mud Ball serves no practical purpose other than to be sold.

Title: Tropical Shell

The Tropical Shell (Japanese: なんごくかいがら Tropical Shell) is a valuable item introduced in Generation VII. The Tropical Shell serves no practical purpose other than to be sold. The Tropcial Shell is a Buddy Souvenir and serves no functional purpose. A Tropical Shell is a possible Souvenir the player's Buddy Pokémon can occasionally collect once their Buddy Level has reached "Ultra Buddy".

Title: Small Bouquet

The Small Bouquet (Japanese: ちいさなはなたば Small Bouquet) is an item introduced in Generation VII. The Small Bouquet is a Buddy Souvenir and serves no functional purpose. A Small Bouquet is a possible Souvenir the player's Buddy Pokémon can occasionally collect once their Buddy Level has reached "Ultra Buddy". In The Discovery, Elaine's partner Pikachu gathered a Small Bouquet and gave it to her Trainer.

Title: Lure

The Lure (Japanese: むしよせコロン Bug Attractant Cologne) is a type of item introduced in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. The Super Lure and Max Lure are stronger versions of this item. When used from the Bag in the overworld, it increases encounter rates of rarer wild Pokémon, including Shiny Pokémon, for 100 steps. It also causes all wild Pokémon spawned to have a level 1 greater than the highest level normally in the area (for example, if an area's wild Pokémon normally range from level 3 to level 5, all wild Pokémon will be level 6 instead). A Lure briefly appeared in The Discovery in Green's Bag.

Title: Super Lure

The Super Lure (Japanese: シルバーコロン Silver Cologne) is a type of item introduced in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. The Lure and Max Lure are alternate versions of this item. When used from the Bag in the overworld, it increases encounter rates of rarer wild Pokémon, including Shiny Pokémon, for 200 steps. It also causes all wild Pokémon spawned to have a level 1 greater than the highest level normally in the area (for example, if an area's wild Pokémon normally range from level 3 to level 5, all wild Pokémon will be level 6 instead).

Title: Max Lure

The Max Lure (Japanese: ゴールドコロン Gold Cologne) is a type of item introduced in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. The Lure and Super Lure are weaker versions of this item. When used from the Bag in the overworld, it increases encounter rates of rarer wild Pokémon, including Shiny Pokémon, for 250 steps. It also causes all wild Pokémon spawned to have a level 1 greater than the highest level normally in the area (for example, if an area's wild Pokémon normally range from level 3 to level 5, all wild Pokémon will be level 6 instead).

Title: Pewter Crunchies

Pewter Crunchies (Japanese: ニビあられ Nibi Arare) are a type of medicine introduced in Generation VII. They are a local specialty of Pewter City and cure a Pokémon of all non-volatile status conditions and confusion. When used from the Bag on a Pokémon, it cures a Pokémon of all non-volatile status conditions (poison, paralysis, sleep, burn, freeze) and confusion. The Pewter Crunchies have been featured in the Ditto and Eevee figures of the Delicious Adventure line by The Pokémon Company International. Pewter Crunchies are the only local specialty item introduced in a different generation than the region they originate from. One of the described side effects of Pewter Crunchies is thirst, as described by Brock and an NPC in the gate between Route 7 and Saffron City. In Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, a book found at Naranja Academy/Uva Academy reveals that Pewter Crunchies have failed to attain big popularity due to their "forbiddingly tough appearance" and "surprisingly thin flavor."

Title: Health Candy

The Health Candy (Japanese: げんきのアメ Vitality Candy) is a type of Candy introduced in Generation VII. When used from the Bag on a Pokémon, it increases that Pokémon's HP by 1 AV, up until it has reached 50 AVs in HP.

Title: Mighty Candy

The Mighty Candy (Japanese: ちからのアメ Power Candy) is a type of Candy introduced in Generation VII. When used from the Bag on a Pokémon, it increases that Pokémon's Attack by 1 AV, up until it has reached 50 AVs in Attack.

Title: Tough Candy

The Tough Candy (Japanese: まもりのアメ Defense Candy) is a type of Candy introduced in Generation VII. When used from the Bag on a Pokémon, it increases that Pokémon's Defense by 1 AV, up until it has reached 50 AVs in Defense.

Title: Smart Candy

The Smart Candy (Japanese: ちしきのアメ Knowledge Candy) is a type of Candy introduced in Generation VII. When used from the Bag on a Pokémon, it increases that Pokémon's Special Attack by 1 AV, up until it has reached 50 AVs in Special Attack.

Title: Courage Candy

The Courage Candy (Japanese: こころのアメ Soul Candy) is a type of Candy introduced in Generation VII. When used from the Bag on a Pokémon, it increases that Pokémon's Special Defense by 1 AV, up until it has reached 50 AVs in Special Defense.

Title: Quick Candy

The Quick Candy (Japanese: はやさのアメ Speed Candy) is a type of Candy introduced in Generation VII. When used from the Bag on a Pokémon, it increases that Pokémon's Speed by 1 AV, up until it has reached 50 AVs in Speed.

Title: Health Candy L

The Health Candy L (Japanese: げんきのアメＬ Vitality Candy L) is a type of Candy introduced in Generation VII. When used from the Bag on a Pokémon Lv. 30 or higher, it increases that Pokémon's HP by 1 AV, up until it has reached 100 AVs in HP.

Title: Mighty Candy L

The Mighty Candy L (Japanese: ちからのアメＬ Power Candy L) is a type of Candy introduced in Generation VII. When used from the Bag on a Lv. 30 or higher Pokémon, it increases that Pokémon's Attack by 1 AV, up until it has reached 100 AVs in Attack.

Title: Tough Candy L

The Tough Candy L (Japanese: まもりのアメＬ Defense Candy L) is a type of Candy in Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!. When used from the Bag on a Lv. 30 or higher Pokémon, it increases that Pokémon's Defense by 1 AV, up until it has reached 100 AVs in Defense.

Title: Smart Candy L

The Smart Candy L (Japanese: ちしきのアメＬ Knowledge Candy L) is a type of Candy introduced in Generation VII. When used from the Bag on a Lv. 30 or higher Pokémon, it increases that Pokémon's Special Attack by 1 AV, up until it has reached 100 AVs in Special Attack.

Title: Courage Candy L

The Courage Candy L (Japanese: こころのアメＬ Soul Candy L) is a type of Candy introduced in Generation VII. When used from the Bag on a Lv. 30 or higher Pokémon, it increases that Pokémon's Special Defense by 1 AV, up until it has reached 100 AVs in Special Defense.

Title: Quick Candy L

The Quick Candy L (Japanese: はやさのアメＬ Speed Candy L) is a type of Candy introduced in Generation VII. When used from the Bag on a Lv. 30 or higher Pokémon, it increases that Pokémon's Speed by 1 AV, up until it has reached 100 AVs in Speed.

Title: Endorsement

The Endorsement (Japanese: すいせんじょう Recommendation) is a Key Item introduced in Generation VIII. Allows the player to participate in the Gym Challenge. In Crackle!! Practice Battle, Henry and Casey sought to impress Leon with their battling skills by driving off a wild Obstagoon in the hopes of him endorsing them for the Gym Challenge. True to their hopes, Leon soon contacted Rose regarding the endorsements he was planning to give to the two Trainers he had just met. In Chomp!! Defeat Drednaw, Henry and Casey's endorsement became widespread knowledge amongst the Gym Leaders, who where surprised to hear that Leon had decided to endorse two Trainers at once. In Blazing!! Bede's Feelings, Bede mentioned that he had been endorsed by Chairman Rose. In Swaying!! Murky Memories, Hop revealed that Leon had offered to endorse him as well, but he had turned his offer down, not wanting to make Leon look bad by possibly performing badly after being endorsed by him.

Title: Wishing Star

Not to be confused with Wishing Piece or Wishing Stone. The Wishing Star (Japanese: ねがいぼし Wishing Star) is a Key Item introduced in Generation VIII. It is a source of Dynamax energy, which can be fit into a Dynamax Band in order to allow a Trainer to Dynamax their Pokémon. According to Professor Magnolia, the Wishing Stars are parts of Eternatus, which is said to have some sort of connection to the Dynamax phenomenon. In Pokémon Sword and Shield, two Wishing Stars fall from the sky near the player and Hop after their battle on Route 2. Professor Magnolia then takes them and fits them into Dynamax Bands for the two. Bede is later revealed to be collecting Wishing Stars for Rose, gathering them from Galar Mine and Galar Mine No. 2. He also attempts to destroy the famous mural in Stow-on-Side to get more of them. During the game's climax, Rose reveals that Macro Cosmos has been giving the gathered Wishing Stars to Eternatus in order to solve the energy crisis that is facing Galar in one thousand years. During the post-game, Sonia is revealed to hold the Wishing Stars confiscated from Chairman Rose after his arrest at her laboratory. They're later stolen by Sordward and Shielbert with the help of Sonia's treacherous assistant in order to advance their plans. In Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl, while the Wishing Star is not an obtainable item, it is mentioned by a Gentleman in Floaroma Town if the player speaks to him with Pokémon Sword or Shield save data on their console. He comments that the player has seen a Wishing Star before and gives them a Jirachi. The Wishing Star is given to Professor Magnolia, who fits it into a Dynamax Band, before giving it back to the player. In Sword and Shield: The Darkest Day!, a Wishing Star crashed down in front of Goh and Sonia at Wedgehurst. Sonia later used it to fashion a Dynamax Band for Goh. A Wishing Star appeared in a flashback in Assistant, being examined by Oleana and a group of Scientists at a Macro Cosmos laboratory. Two Wishing Stars appeared in Ooh Shiny!! Legendary Equipment. One year before the arc's events, Casey and Henry saw two Wishing Stars falling into the Slumbering Weald, and entered the forest to find them. With the help of Henry's Farfetch'd, they eventually found them, and, as thanks for the help, Casey gave one of them to Henry. They were later fitted into Dynamax Bands for the two Trainers. In Watch Out!! Strong Rivals, Bede found three Wishing Stars after his Hatenna crushed a boulder that had been blocking the entrance to the Galar Mine. In Stealth!! Infiltrate the Energy Plant, Rose and Oleana were seen taking multiple Wishing Stars to Eternatus at the Energy Plant.

Title: Dynamax Band

The Dynamax Band (Japanese: ダイマックスＢ Daimax Band) is a Key Item introduced in Generation VIII. It was invented by Oleana and allows a Trainer's Pokémon to Dynamax or Gigantamax in Power Spots and perform Max Moves. Allows the player's Pokémon to Dynamax or Gigantamax in Power Spots and perform Max Moves and G-Max Moves, respectively. Each Trainer can only Dynamax or Gigantamax a Pokémon once per battle. On September 4, 2024, in conjunction with the release of Dynamax in Pokémon GO, the Dynamax Band was added to the game as a reward for completing the Special Research campaign, To the Max!. However, unlike the core series games, it is not required to Dynamax a Pokémon; rather, it is merely for cosmetic purposes. Dynamax Bands first appeared in Flash of the Titans!, under the possession of Lance and Leon. Lance used his Dynamax Band to Dynamax his Gyarados during the World Coronation Series finals, while Leon used his own band to Gigantamax his Charizard. Leon can also use his Dynamax Band to Gigantamax his Rillaboom, as shown in Curtain Up! Fight the Fights!, and his Cinderace, as seen in Partners in Time!, while Lance was seen using his Dynamax Band to also Dynamax his Dragonite in Pride of a Champion!. In The Climb to Be the Very Best!, Leon gave Ash a Dynamax Band of his own so that he could Gigantamax his Pikachu during their battle. After the battle, Leon let Ash keep the Dynamax Band. Starting from The Winding Path to Greatness!, Ash also became able to use his Dynamax Band to Gigantamax his Gengar. A Dynamax Band belonging to Raihan first appeared in Toughing It Out!. He uses it to Gigantamax his Duraludon. In Sword and Shield: The Darkest Day!, Sonia fashioned a Wishing Star she and Goh had found into a Dynamax Band, which she gave to Goh. He later used it to Dynamax his Raboot in a battle against one of Oleana's subordinates, who had used his own Dynamax Band to Gigantamax his Garbodor. In Ultra Exciting from the Shocking Start!, Bea was revealed to own a Dynamax Band. She was first seen using it in A Battle of Mega Versus Max!, Gigantamaxing her Machamp during her third World Coronation Series battle against Ash. In The Sweet Taste of Battle!, a Dynamax Band appeared in Opal's possession. She used it to Gigantamax her Alcremie during her battle with Ash. In Battling Turned Up to Eleven!, Marnie was shown to own a Dynamax Band. She used it to Gigantamax her Grimmsnarl during her World Coronation Series match against Ash. In Valor: A Strategic Part of Battling!, Cynthia was shown to own a Dynamax Band. She used it to Dynamax her Togekiss during her Masters Eight Tournament battle against Ash. In The Bittersweet Truth, a Dynamax Band appeared in Kabu's possession. Victor, Leon, and Hop's Dynamax Bands appeared in The Champion. Victor used his Dynamax Band to catch Eternatus. Two Dynamax Bands first appeared in Zap!! A Rising Beam of Light, under the ownership of Henry and Casey, respectively. Henry was first seen using his Dynamax Band to catch a Gurdurr during a Max Raid Battle. In Ooh Shiny!! Legendary Equipment, it was revealed that these two Dynamax Bands had been made from Wishing Stars the two Pokédex holders had found a year earlier in the Slumbering Weald. Like in the games, each Galar Gym Leader, excluding Piers, also owns a Dynamax Band. The same also applies to Hop, Bede, Marnie, Leon, Oleana, and Rose. Dynamax Bands first appeared in MTM03, under the possession of Suguru Tsukino and Kira Hoshio. They were seen again in the following chapter. From MTM07 onward, Kira used his Dynamax Band regularly. In MTM11, Kira was seen to have upgraded to a Dynamax Band + (Japanese: ダイマックスバンド＋(プラス) Daimax Band +). The Dynamax Band has been featured in several officially licensed figures and goods.

Title: Rotom Bike

The Rotom Bike (Japanese: ロトムじてんしゃ Rotom Bike) is a Key Item introduced in Generation VIII. The Rotom Bike allows the player to travel faster on land and includes a boost that temporarily boosts the speed even further, but requires a recharge period afterwards before it can be used again. By talking to a Watt Trader in the Wild Area, the player can improve the Rotom Bike three times in exchange of Watts (1,000, 3,000, and 5,000), thus allowing for the recharge period to take less time to complete. After being upgraded with Water Mode, it also allows them to ride on water. In The Isle of Armor, the player can also change their bike's appearance to a "Sparkling White" or "Glistening Black" design, both of which come with particle effects resembling Shiny Pokémon and fully upgraded speed. The player can switch between the base Water Mode and the two alternatives at any time. In Gulp!! Gulp Pokémon, Henry was revealed to have obtained a Rotom Bike from a man after dealing with the Team Yell Grunts who had been causing him trouble. In Swaying!! Murky Memories, Casey was revealed to have upgraded Henry's Rotom Bike to make it able to travel on water, allowing him to cross Circhester Bay with it. Rotom Bike was introduced as an Item card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game during the English Sword & Shield Series (the Japanese Sword & Shield Era). It was first released in the Japanese V Starter Set Lightning. It later appeared in the Japanese Sword expansion before debuting in English in the Sword & Shield expansion, with artwork by 5ban Graphics. It allows the player to draw cards until they have six in their hand, then their turn ends. The Rotom Bike is implied to be produced by Macro Cosmos Tech through a poster in Motostoke.

Title: Sausages

Sausages (Japanese: あらびきヴルスト Coarse Ground Sausages) is a type of ingredient introduced in Generation VIII.

Title: Mixed Mushrooms

Mixed Mushrooms (Japanese: キノコパック Mushroom Pack) is a type of ingredient introduced in Generation VIII.

Title: Large Leek

Large Leek (Japanese: ふといながねぎ Large Scallion) is a type of ingredient introduced in Generation VIII. Large Leek can be used as an ingredient in cooking, with a base Strength of 185. It can be used to cook the following recipes: Large Leek can be gathered by the player's helper Pokémon. Large Leek has been featured in the artwork on a keychain that was released as part of the Pokémon Yurutto goods collection that was exclusive to the Pokémon Center.

Title: Fancy Apple

Fancy Apple (Japanese: とくせんリンゴ Select Apple) is a type of ingredient introduced in Generation VIII. Fancy Apple can be used as an ingredient in cooking, with a base Strength of 90. It can be used to cook the following recipes: Fancy Apple can be gathered by the player's helper Pokémon.

Title: Brittle Bones

Brittle Bones (Japanese: かぼそいホネ Delicate Bones) is a type of ingredient introduced in Generation VIII.

Title: Pungent Root

Pungent Root (Japanese: みずべのハーブ Waterside Herb) is a type of ingredient introduced in Generation VIII. The Pungent Root resembles the wasabi root, which is grated into a paste and used as a condiment in Japanese cuisine, such as sushi. An ingredient known as Wasabi was introduced in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet.

Title: Fried Food

Fried Food (Japanese: フライもりあわせ Fried Assortment) is a type of ingredient introduced in Generation VIII.

Title: Camping Gear

The Camping Gear (Japanese: キャンプセット Camp Set) is a Key Item introduced in Generation VIII. The Camping Gear allows the player to set up a Pokémon Camp. It cannot be used on water, in buildings, or while challenging a Gym, the Champion Cup tournament, or the Galarian Star Tournament. Camping Gear was introduced as an Item card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game during the English Sword & Shield Series (the Japanese Sword & Shield Era). It was first released in the Japanese Single Strike Master expansion and the English Battle Styles expansion, with artwork by sadaji. It allows the player to search for a card from their deck and place it in their hand, at the cost of having to end their turn immediately.

Title: Rusted Sword

The Rusted Sword (Japanese: くちたけん Rusted Sword) is a held item introduced in Generation VIII. It is a legendary artifact associated with Zacian. During the climax of Pokémon Sword, the player's Rusted Sword, along with Hop's Rusted Shield, summons Zacian and Zamazenta to help the two Trainers to battle against Eternamax Eternatus at the tower summit. The Rusted Sword temporarily becomes Zacian's held item for the duration of this battle. During the post-game, the player returns the Rusted Sword to Slumbering Weald. It is soon after temporarily stolen by Sordward, before the player defeats him in a battle and retrieves the stolen item. Before the player battles Zacian at the tower summit in order to catch it, they give the Rusted Sword to it as its held item. In Pokémon Shield, the Rusted Sword is instead obtained by Hop. During the post-game, when he returns it to the altar at Slumbering Weald, it is stolen by Sordward. Hop battles Sordward, but is defeated, allowing him to get away with the item. Later, Sordward uses the Rusted Sword to lure Zacian to the tower summit. After the player has battled Zacian, it flees, and Hop retrieves the Rusted Sword from Sordward, before chasing after Zacian. After Hop has caught Zacian at Slumbering Weald, the Rusted Sword becomes its held item in the subsequent battles he participates in. When a Zacian is holding this item in battle, it transforms into its Crowned Sword form. In-battle, it also changes Zacian's Iron Head into Behemoth Blade. In Pokémon UNITE, the Rusted Sword is a held item that has no effect. Zacian is forced to use it; no other Pokémon can have it. Essentially, it is a balancing limitation, only allowing Zacian to have two useful held items instead of three. Players start the game with the Rusted Sword in their Item Box, even before they have access to Zacian. In Sword and Shield: "From Here to Eternatus!", Goh and Sonia found the Rusted Sword at the altar in the Slumbering Weald after requesting the help of Zacian and Zamazenta in dealing with the Darkest Day. Goh collected it and the Rusted Shield and took both items with him. In Sword and Shield... The Legends Awaken!, the Rusted Sword called forth Zacian to the tower summit, who grabbed the Sword in its mouth, allowing it to transform into its Crowned Sword form. After Goh caught Eternatus, Zacian left its Sword behind. Ash and Goh were later seen returning the Rusted Sword and Shield where they once rested. The Rusted Sword appeared in a flashback in The Champion, under the possession of Victor. When he held it up during his and Hop's battle against Eternatus at the tower summit, it summoned Zacian to their aid and allowed it to transform into its Crowned Sword form. The Rusted Sword first appeared in a flashback in Ooh Shiny!! Legendary Equipment, along with the Rusted Shield. One year earlier, Henry, accompanied by Casey, entered Slumbering Weald in order to see the two legendary artifacts. After confronting illusions of Zacian and Zamazenta at the altar at the end of the forest and convincing them they weren't trying to steal anything, the two Trainers approached the two artifacts on the altar, but were then knocked out by a bright burst of light. When they recovered, the artifacts were gone. In Cracking!! Brilliant Tap-Dancing, it was revealed that the artifacts had been stolen by Sordward and Shielbert. In Cracked!! The Rusted Sword and Shield, they used the artifacts to "prove" themselves as descendants of Galar's ancient heroes. When the two brothers took on Eternatus at the tower summit, they took out the artifacts, but nothing happened, and the items were sent flying to the moat surrounding the Hammerlocke Stadium, from which they were recovered by Casey's Falinks and Stunfisk and taken to her, who gave them to Henry to clean and repair. However, to his horror, Henry soon realized that he could not forge and repair the artifacts. In Oh My!! The Disappearance of Henry, the artifacts, along with Henry, disappeared as Zacian and Zamazenta attacked Eternatus, getting teleported away by an unseen Pokémon. Casey later discovered that the artifacts had wound up in two different places: the Isle of Armor and the Crown Tundra. In Clip-Clop!! The King's Horse, Bede mentioned he had detected signs of the Rusted Sword on Spectrier. In Rumble!! The Darkest Day Returns, he detected signs of the artifact at Spectrier's barn at the Crown Shrine, but the artifact wasn't there. Once Calyrex had tamed its steed, it told Spectrier to take it to the artifact. After finding it, Calyrex brought it to Henry at the Crown Shrine, allowing him to purify it of Eternatus's poison. In Shing!! Final Form, Zacian used the sword to transform to its Crowned Sword form. The Rusted Sword was introduced as a Pokémon Tool card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game during the English Sword & Shield Series (the Japanese Sword & Shield Era). It was first released in the Japanese Shiny Star V expansion before debuting in English in the Shining Fates expansion, with artwork by 5ban Graphics. It allows Zacian V to deal 30 more damage to the opponent's Active Pokémon. The Rusted Sword appears to feature fictional runic characters, likely a combination of both Japanese and Old Norse characters. While the player seemingly obtains the item in Slumbering Weald during the climax of Pokémon Sword, this is not the case, as the game simply tells the player they obtained it without them actually obtaining anything.

Title: Rusted Shield

The Rusted Shield (Japanese: くちたたて Rusted Shield) is a held item introduced in Generation VIII. It is a legendary artifact associated with Zamazenta. During the climax of Pokémon Shield, the player's Rusted Shield, along with Hop's Rusted Sword, summons Zamazenta and Zacian to help the two Trainers to battle against Eternamax Eternatus at the tower summit. The Rusted Shield temporarily becomes Zamazenta's held item for the duration of this battle. During the post-game, the player returns the Rusted Shield to Slumbering Weald. It is soon after temporarily stolen by Shielbert, before the player defeats him in a battle and retrieves the stolen item. Before the player battles Zamazenta at the tower summit in order to catch it, they give the Rusted Shield to it as its held item. In Pokémon Sword, the Rusted Shield is instead obtained by Hop. During the post-game, when he returns it to the altar at Slumbering Weald, it is stolen by Shielbert. Hop battles Shielbert, but is defeated, allowing him to get away with the item. Later, Shielbert uses the Rusted Shield to lure Zamazenta to the tower summit. After the player has battled Zamazenta, it flees, and Hop retrieves the Rusted Shield from Shielbert, before chasing after Zamazenta. After Hop has caught Zamazenta at Slumbering Weald, the Rusted Shield becomes its held item in the subsequent battles he participates in. When a Zamazenta is holding this item in battle, it transforms into its Crowned Shield form. In-battle, it also changes Zamazenta's Iron Head into Behemoth Bash. In Sword and Shield: "From Here to Eternatus!", Goh and Sonia found the Rusted Shield at the altar in the Slumbering Weald after requesting the help of Zacian and Zamazenta in dealing with the Darkest Day. Goh collected it and the Rusted Sword and took both items with him. In Sword and Shield... The Legends Awaken!, the Rusted Shield called forth Zamazenta to the tower summit, who grabbed the Shield in its mouth, allowing it to transform into its Crowned Shield form. After Goh caught Eternatus, Zamazenta left its Shield behind. Ash and Goh were later seen returning the Rusted Sword and Shield where they once rested. The Rusted Shield appeared in a flashback in The Champion, under the possession of Hop. When he held it up during his and Victor's battle against Eternatus at the tower summit, it summoned Zamazenta to their aid and allowed it to transform into its Crowned Shield form. The Rusted Shield first appeared in a flashback in Ooh Shiny!! Legendary Equipment, along with the Rusted Sword. One year earlier, Henry, accompanied by Casey, entered Slumbering Weald in order to see the two legendary artifacts. After confronting illusions of Zacian and Zamazenta at the altar at the end of the forest and convincing them they weren't trying to steal anything, the two Trainers approached the two artifacts on the altar, but were then knocked out by a bright burst of light. When they recovered, the artifacts were gone. In Cracking!! Brilliant Tap-Dancing, it was revealed that the artifacts had been stolen by Sordward and Shielbert. In Cracked!! The Rusted Sword and Shield, they used the artifacts to "prove" themselves as descendants of Galar's ancient heroes. When the two brothers took on Eternatus at the tower summit, they took out the artifacts, but nothing happened, and the items were sent flying to the moat surrounding the Hammerlocke Stadium, from which they were recovered by Casey's Falinks and Stunfisk and taken to her, who gave them to Henry to clean and repair. However, to his horror, Henry soon realized that he could not forge and repair the artifacts. He refuses to hand over the artifacts, stating that he only joined the Gym Challenge because he wanted to obtain them. In Oh My!! The Disappearance of Henry, the artifacts, along with Henry, disappeared as Zacian and Zamazenta attacked Eternatus, getting teleported away by an unseen Pokémon. Casey later discovered that the artifacts had wound up in two different places: the Isle of Armor and the Crown Tundra. In Crackle!! Retrieve the Sword & Shield, the Rusted Shield was revealed to have been found by Galarian Moltres and hidden at Loop Lagoon. Henry was able to reclaim the artifact from Moltres shortly before it grabbed him and took him to the Crown Tundra, where he later successfully purified it of Eternatus's poison. In Shing!! Final Form, Zamazenta used the shield to transform to its Crowned Shield form. The Rusted Shield was introduced as a Pokémon Tool card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game during the English Sword & Shield Series (the Japanese Sword & Shield Era). It was first released in the Japanese Shiny Star V expansion before debuting in English in the Shining Fates expansion, with artwork by 5ban Graphics. It allows Zamazenta V to gain 70 HP. While the player seemingly obtains this item in Slumbering Weald during the climax of Pokémon Shield, this is not the case, as the game simply tells the player they obtained it without them actually obtaining anything.

Title: Fossilized Bird

The Fossilized Bird (Japanese: カセキのトリ Fossilized Bird) is a fossil introduced in Generation VIII. It is the counterpart of the Fossilized Fish. If brought to one of the following locations alongside a Fossilized Drake or Fossilized Dino, the Fossilized Bird can be regenerated into a Dracozolt or Arctozolt, respectively. A Fossilized Bird appeared in A Pinch of This, a Pinch of That!, where Ash, Goh, and Chloe helped Cara Liss and Bray Zenn to dig it up in the Wild Area. It was later combined with a Fossilized Dino and restored into an Arctozolt. A Fossilized Bird appeared in Clip-Clop!! The King's Horse, where Marnie was shown to discovered one for Cara Liss. Combined with the Fossilized Drake that Marnie had also found, Cara Liss was able to restore it into a Dracozolt. A Fossilized Bird is featured in the Pokémon Trading Card Game on the Rare Fossil Item card included in the Darkness Ablaze expansion, first released in the Japanese Infinity Zone expansion. It is a Fossil Trainer card, so after it is played it is put onto the Bench as a Colorless-type Basic Pokémon. It can evolve into any of the Generation VIII Fossil Pokémon: Dracozolt, Arctozolt, Dracovish, or Arctovish. The Pokémon revived from the Fossilized Bird have 10 extra points in their Attack and Special Attack Stats, and 10 fewer points in their Defense and Special Defense stats, compared to their counterparts revived from the Fossilized Fish.

Title: Fossilized Fish

The Fossilized Fish (Japanese: カセキのサカナ Fossilized Fish) is a fossil introduced in Generation VIII. It is the counterpart of the Fossilized Bird. If brought to one of the following locations alongside a Fossilized Drake or Fossilized Dino, the Fossilized Fish can be regenerated into a Dracovish or Arctovish, respectively. In Pokémon the Series, the appearances of Fossilized Drake and Fish are swapped, making them match the Pokémon they are supposed to restore. A Fossilized Fish appeared in A Pinch of This, a Pinch of That!, where Ash, Goh, and Chloe helped Cara Liss and Bray Zenn to dig it up in the Wild Area. It was later combined with a Fossilized Drake and restored into a Dracovish. A Fossilized Fish appeared in Clip-Clop!! The King's Horse, where Marnie was shown to discovered one for Cara Liss. Combined with the Fossilized Dino that Marnie had also found, Cara Liss was able to restore it into an Arctovish. A Fossilized Fish is featured in the Pokémon Trading Card Game on the Rare Fossil Item card included in the Darkness Ablaze expansion, first released in the Japanese Infinity Zone expansion. It is a Fossil Trainer card, so after it is played it is put onto the Bench as a Colorless-type Basic Pokémon. It can evolve into any of the Generation VIII Fossil Pokémon: Dracozolt, Arctozolt, Dracovish, or Arctovish. In Pokémon Sword and Shield, the Fossilized Fish and Fossilized Drake appear to have mistakenly swapped sprites. The Fossilized Fish offers the "-vish" head for Dracovish and Arctovish but visually looks like the body provided by the Fossilized Drake. In the animated series, their appearances are the opposite of their game sprites. The Pokémon revived from the Fossilized Fish have 10 extra points in their Defense and Special Defense Stats, and 10 fewer points in their Attack and Special Attack stats, compared to their counterparts revived from the Fossilized Bird.

Title: Fossilized Drake

The Fossilized Drake (Japanese: カセキのリュウ Fossilized Dragon) is a fossil introduced in Generation VIII. It is the counterpart of the Fossilized Dino. If brought to one of the following locations alongside a Fossilized Bird or Fossilized Fish, the Fossilized Drake can be regenerated into a Dracozolt or Dracovish, respectively. In Pokémon the Series, the appearances of Fossilized Fish and Drake are swapped, making them match the Pokémon they are supposed to restore. A Fossilized Drake appeared in A Pinch of This, a Pinch of That!, where Ash, Goh, and Chloe helped Cara Liss and Bray Zenn to dig it up in the Wild Area. It was later combined with a Fossilized Fish and restored into a Dracovish. A Fossilized Drake appeared in Clip-Clop!! The King's Horse, where Marnie was shown to discovered one for Cara Liss. Combined with the Fossilized Bird that Marnie had also found, Cara Liss was able to restore it into a Dracozolt. A Fossilized Drake is featured in the Pokémon Trading Card Game on the Rare Fossil Item card included in the Darkness Ablaze expansion, first released in the Japanese Infinity Zone expansion. It is a Fossil Trainer card, so after it is played it is put onto the Bench as a Colorless-type Basic Pokémon. It can evolve into any of the Generation VIII Fossil Pokémon: Dracozolt, Arctozolt, Dracovish, or Arctovish. In Pokémon Sword and Shield, the Fossilized Fish and Fossilized Drake appear to have mistakenly swapped sprites. The Fossilized Drake offers the "Draco-" body for Dracozolt and Dracovish but visually looks like the head provided by the Fossilized Fish. In the animated series, their appearances are the opposite of their game sprites. The Pokémon revived from the Fossilized Drake have 10 fewer points in their Special Attack and Special Defense stats, and 20 extra points in Speed, compared to their counterparts revived from the Fossilized Dino.

Title: Fossilized Dino

The Fossilized Dino (Japanese: カセキのクビナガ Fossilized Plesiosaur) is a fossil introduced in Generation VIII. It is the counterpart of the Fossilized Drake. If brought to one of the following locations alongside a Fossilized Bird or Fossilized Fish, the Fossilized Dino can be regenerated into an Arctozolt or Arctovish, respectively. A Fossilized Dino appeared in A Pinch of This, a Pinch of That!, where Ash, Goh, and Chloe helped Cara Liss and Bray Zenn to dig it up in the Wild Area. It was later combined with a Fossilized Bird and restored into an Arctozolt. A Fossilized Dino appeared in Clip-Clop!! The King's Horse, where Marnie was shown to discovered one for Cara Liss. Combined with the Fossilized Fish that Marnie had also found, Cara Liss was able to restore it into an Arctovish. A Fossilized Dino is featured in the Pokémon Trading Card Game on the Rare Fossil Item card included in the Darkness Ablaze expansion, first released in the Japanese Infinity Zone expansion. It is a Fossil Trainer card, so after it is played it is put onto the Bench as a Colorless-type Basic Pokémon. It can evolve into any of the Generation VIII Fossil Pokémon: Dracozolt, Arctozolt, Dracovish, or Arctovish. Despite the English name being "Dino", plesiosaurs are not actually dinosaurs; they are marine reptiles. The Pokémon revived from the Fossilized Dino have 10 extra points in their Special Attack and Special Defense stats, and 20 fewer points in Speed, compared to their counterparts revived from the Fossilized Drake.

Title: Strawberry Sweet

The Strawberry Sweet (Japanese: いちごアメざいく Strawberry Amezaiku) is a type of held item introduced in Generation VIII. It is one of the sweets that can be used to evolve Milcery. Causes Milcery to evolve into Alcremie with a Strawberry Sweet when the player does a spin while Milcery is holding it. This consumes the Strawberry Sweet. A Strawberry Sweet first appeared in The Sweet Taste of Battle!, decorating a cupcake that Goh was eating. When his newly caught Milcery showed interest in it, he placed it on her head and then spun around with her to celebrate making a new friend, inadvertently triggering her Evolution into Alcremie in the process. Four Strawberry Sweets appeared in a flashback in The Bittersweet Truth. Murdock's Milcery grabbed one of them and spun around with it, triggering her Evolution into Alcremie.

Title: Love Sweet

The Love Sweet (Japanese: ハートアメざいく Heart Amezaiku) is a type of held item introduced in Generation VIII. It is one of the sweets that can be used to evolve Milcery. Causes Milcery to evolve into Alcremie with a Love Sweet when the player does a spin while Milcery is holding it. This consumes the Love Sweet.

Causes Milcery to evolve into Alcremie with a Berry Sweet when the player does a spin while Milcery is holding it. This consumes the Berry Sweet.

Causes Milcery to evolve into Alcremie with a Clover Sweet when the player does a spin while Milcery is holding it. This consumes the Clover Sweet.

Causes Milcery to evolve into Alcremie with a Flower Sweet when the player does a spin while Milcery is holding it. This consumes the Flower Sweet.

Causes Milcery to evolve into Alcremie with a Star Sweet when the player does a spin while Milcery is holding it. This consumes the Star Sweet.

Title: Ribbon Sweet

The Ribbon Sweet (Japanese: リボンアメざいく Ribbon Amezaiku) is a type of held item introduced in Generation VIII. It is one of the sweets that can be used to evolve Milcery. Causes Milcery to evolve into Alcremie with a Ribbon Sweet when the player does a spin while Milcery is holding it. This consumes the Ribbon Sweet.

Title: Sweet Apple

The Sweet Apple (Japanese: あまーいりんご Sweet Apple) is a type of item introduced in Generation VIII. Causes Applin to evolve into Appletun upon use. This consumes the Sweet Apple. Sweet Apples were added to Pokémon GO on April 24, 2025. Sweet Apples are required to evolve certain Pokémon in Pokémon GO. They are consumed upon use. Applin requires 200 Applin Candy and 20 Sweet Apples to evolve into Appletun. Tapping apples that appear near PokéStops can award either Sweet Apples, Tart Apples, or an encounter with a wild Applin. Apples will also have an increased chance of appearing at PokéStops with Mossy Lure Modules. A Sweet Apple is seen in the artwork for the Pokémon School merchandise collection exclusive to the Pokémon Center.

Title: Tart Apple

The Tart Apple (Japanese: すっぱいりんご Sour Apple) is a type of item introduced in Generation VIII. Causes Applin to evolve into Flapple upon use. This consumes the Tart Apple. Tart Apples were added to Pokémon GO on April 24, 2025. Tart Apples are required to evolve certain Pokémon in Pokémon GO. They are consumed upon use. Applin requires 200 Applin Candy and 20 Tart Apples to evolve into Flapple. Tapping apples that appear near PokéStops can award either Tart Apples, Sweet Apples, or an encounter with a wild Applin. Apples will also have an increased chance of appearing at PokéStops with Mossy Lure Modules. The Tart Apple has been featured in several officially licensed merchandise.

Title: Throat Spray

Throat Spray (Japanese: のどスプレー Throat Spray) is a type of held item introduced in Generation VIII. After the Pokémon holding Throat Spray uses a sound-based move, it consumes the item and its Special Attack is raised by one stage. Throat Spray is not used if the move fails, or finishes the battle. Despite Pokémon being stated to be unable to use manmade healing items when held, the appearance of Throat Spray is similar to the appearance of spray-type Potions.

Title: Eject Pack

The Eject Pack (Japanese: だっしゅつパック Break-Out Pack) is a type of consumable held item introduced in Generation VIII. It switches out the holder if any of its stats are lowered. If any of the holder's stats is lowered (including by the use of its own moves such as Overheat), the Eject Pack is consumed and the holder automatically switches out. If the holder is switched in while Sticky Web is set on its side of the field, it will immediately switch out again. Because only one Pokémon can switch out per move, a Pokémon's held Eject Pack will not activate if it is hit by Parting Shot. If multiple Pokémon's held Eject Buttons and Eject Packs would activate at once, only the held item of the fastest among these Pokémon will activate.

Title: Blunder Policy

Blunder Policy (Japanese: からぶりほけん Miss Insurance) is a type of held item introduced in Generation VIII. If one of the holder's moves misses one of its targets because of an accuracy or evasion check, the Blunder Policy is consumed and the holder's Speed is increased by two stages. Blunder Policy does not activate if a one-hit knockout move misses, if Triple Kick, Triple Axel, or Population Bomb ends early by failing one of their subsequent accuracy checks, or if a move misses due to a target being in the semi-invulnerable turn of a move. The Blunder Policy only activates if the move misses, not for other reasons it might fail to affect a target (e.g. immunity due to type or Ability, being protected by a protection move, etc.). If Dragon Darts would have missed one of its targets in a Double Battle for accuracy or evasion reasons, but the other target is successfully hit with the move, that target will be hit twice, and the Blunder Policy will still activate even though no message about missing a target ever appears. Main article: Blunder Policy (Brilliant Stars 131) Blunder Policy was introduced as a Pokémon Tool card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game during the English Sword & Shield Series (the Japanese Sword & Shield Era). It was first released in the Japanese S-P Promotional cards and the English Brilliant Stars expansion, with artwork by Ayaka Yoshida. It allows the player to draw three cards if the player lands on tails when flipping a coin for an attack of the Pokémon it is attached to. A set of 2 memos designed after the Weakness Policy and Blunder Policy has been released on December 12, 2024 and is exclusive to the Pokémon Center.

Title: Room Service

Room Service (Japanese: ルームサービス Room Service) is a type of consumable held item introduced in Generation VIII. It lowers the holder's Speed in Trick Room. If the move Trick Room is used while a Pokémon is holding Room Service, the Room Service is consumed, causing the holder's Speed to drop by one stage. Additionally, Room Service will also be consumed if a Pokémon holding it switches in while Trick Room is already in effect, similar to Electric Seed and the other terrain seeds.

Title: Utility Umbrella

The Utility Umbrella (Japanese: ばんのうがさ Utility Umbrella) is a type of held item introduced in Generation VIII. The Pokémon holding the Utility Umbrella is unaffected by effects of rain and harsh sunlight. All effects of sandstorm, hail, and snow work as normal. The holder's Abilities and self-targeting moves behave as if there was no rain or sunlight present (with the exceptions of Protosynthesis and Orichalcum Pulse). For example, if the holder has Chlorophyll or uses Growth or Synthesis in harsh sunlight, it will not have its Speed doubled, boost stats by only 1 stage, and heal only half of its HP, respectively. Water-type and Fire-type moves that target the holder do not have their power increased or decreased in rain or sun. Moves with a different accuracy in rain or sun (such as Thunder) have their normal accuracy when they target the holder. Conversely, these weather effects are applied as normal to the holder's moves. In rain or harsh sunlight, Solar Beam's number of turns to charge and power is unaffected by weather if used by a Pokémon holding the Utility Umbrella. During rain or harsh sunlight, the holder's Weather Ball will be Normal-type and its power will not be doubled (as if no weather was present). During harsh sunlight, Hydro Steam's power is not boosted by 50% if used by a Pokémon holding the Utility Umbrella, but is instead reduced by 50% like other Water-type moves. These effects are only removed if the move's user is holding a Utility Umbrella; they are not affected by a target's held Utility Umbrella. A Pokémon holding the Utility Umbrella can be frozen during harsh sunlight. Overcast Form Cherrim holding the Utility Umbrella cannot change to Sunshine Form. If a Sunshine Form Cherrim gains the Utility Umbrella (through a move such as Trick), it will immediately change to Overcast Form. Likewise, a Cherrim losing the Utility Umbrella in the sun will immediately change to Sunshine Form. A Pokémon holding a Utility Umbrella is still affected by its allies' Flower Gift, however. The Utility Umbrella has no effect outside of battle, so Sliggoo can still evolve even when holding the Utility Umbrella.

Title: Dynamax Candy

The Dynamax Candy (Japanese: ダイマックスアメ Daimax Candy) is a type of Candy introduced in Generation VIII. It increases the Dynamax Level of a Pokémon. When used from the Bag on a Pokémon, a Dynamax Candy increases its Dynamax Level by one. It can be used on a Pokémon until its maximum Dynamax Level of 10 is reached. Each Dynamax Level increases the HP of a Dynamax Pokémon by 5% of its maximum base HP. Dynamax Candy cannot be used on Zacian, Zamazenta, and Eternatus due to their inability to Dynamax. However, it can be used on Shedinja, despite not having any effect since its HP does not increase when it Dynamaxes.

Title: Lonely Mint

The Lonely Mint (Japanese: さみしがりミント Lonely Mint) is an item introduced in Generation VIII. It is one of the types of mints that change the effect of a Pokémon's Nature. If used from the Bag, it changes the effect of a Pokémon's Nature on its stats to that of the Lonely Nature, increasing its Attack stat and decreasing its Defense stat.

Title: Adamant Mint

If used from the Bag, it changes the effect of a Pokémon's Nature on its stats to that of the Adamant Nature, increasing its Attack stat and decreasing its Special Attack stat.

Title: Wishing Piece

Not to be confused with Wishing Star or Wishing Stone. A Wishing Piece (Japanese: ねがいのかたまり Wishing Clump) is an item introduced in Generation VIII. A Wishing Piece can be thrown into an inactive Pokémon Den (except the giant den in the Watchtower Ruins or Regigigas's den in the Giant's Bed) in the Wild Area, Isle of Armor, or Crown Tundra in order to enable a Max Raid Battle at said den.

Title: Cracked Pot

The Cracked Pot (Japanese: われたポット Cracked Pot) is a type of item introduced in Generation VIII. Causes Phony Form Sinistea to evolve into Phony Form Polteageist upon use. This consumes the item.

Title: Chipped Pot

The Chipped Pot (Japanese: かけたポット Chipped Pot) is a type of item introduced in Generation VIII. Causes Antique Form Sinistea to evolve into Antique Form Polteageist upon use. This consumes the item.

Title: Moomoo Cheese

Moomoo Cheese (Japanese: モーモーチーズ Moomoo Cheese) is a type of ingredient introduced in Generation VIII. Moomoo Cheese was introduced as an Item card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game during the English Sword & Shield Series (the Japanese Sword & Shield Era). It was first released in the Japanese Legendary Heartbeat subset and the English Vivid Voltage expansion, with artwork by 5ban Graphics. It allows the player to heal 30 damage from up to two Pokémon that have Energy attached. As its name suggests, it may be made from Moomoo Milk.

Title: Spice Mix

Spice Mix (Japanese: スパイスセット Spice Set) is a type of ingredient introduced in Generation VIII. The Spice Mix has been featured in the artwork on a keychain that was released as part of the Pokémon Yurutto goods collection that was exclusive to the Pokémon Center.
