Title: Ketchup

Ketchup (Japanese: ケチャップ Ketchup) is a type of ingredient introduced in Generation IX. It can be used as an ingredient in making a sandwich. It adds the following modifiers to a sandwich it is used on:

Title: Mustard

If you were looking for the sandwich ingredient, see Mustard (item). Mustard (Japanese: マスタード Mustard) is a character in The Isle of Armor in Pokémon Sword and Shield. He is a former Champion of the Galar region. He is the only character in the games to have the Trainer class Dojo Master (Japanese: どうじょうぬし Dojo Master). In the past, Mustard used to be a Fighting-type Gym Leader. He became the Galar League Champion soon after a heated battle with Opal about 50 years prior to the events of Sword and Shield, a position which he managed to hold for 18 years, longer than any other League Champion before or since. He only lost the title after his prowess in battle began to wane following the death of his partner Pokémon. After refusing a suggestion by the then chairman of the Galar Pokémon League to have a match rigged in his favor, Mustard retired from the Galar League entirely and traveled around the world, until he bought an unnamed island off the coast of Galar and named it the Isle of Armor, setting up his Master Dojo there to teach people about Pokémon battles. One of his students was Leon, who would go on to become a Champion himself one day as well, despite failing to finish Mustard's training due to his horrible sense of direction preventing him from finding the Towers of Two Fists. Upon arriving at the Master Dojo, Mustard accepts the player as his student and has an easy battle with them. He then puts all of his students through three trials, with the player ending up as the winner and earning a Kubfu as a gift from Mustard. After befriending Kubfu, the player must evolve it at either the Tower of Darkness or the Tower of Waters, depending on their choice, with Mustard acting as the final opponent before Kubfu can evolve into Urshifu. Once the player has successfully found a way to unlock Urshifu's Gigantamax potential, Mustard will challenge the player to one more battle, where he holds nothing back. After this battle, he can be challenged to a rematch once a day if the player talks to him while he plays Pokémon Quest in the Master Dojo. This is a transcript of the back of Mustard's League Cards in Pokémon Sword and Shield. "Mustard is the former Fighting-type Gym Leader and is regarded as one of the strongest Champions in Galar's history—reigning undefeated for 18 years. His intense drive for strength meant he insisted on discipline, both in himself and in others. His character changed drastically, however, after his post-retirement globe-trotting. His sudden purchase of a deserted island added to the surprise of those around him. When asked for his reasoning, he answered with a smile, "There are lots of ways to be strong in this world. I want to see them all!" The name "Isle of Armor" was also his idea." "This photo was taken about 50 years ago, when Mustard defeated the Fairy-type Gym Leader and went on to become Champion. He reigned undefeated until he lost his partner Pokémon. After this, his losses started to mount. He remained popular even after losing the mantle of Champion, but decided to retire after the league chairman at that time asked him to win in a rigged match. Mustard developed an obsession with video games after his retirement. Apparently he held off while he was still active in order to maintain his image to his fans." Mustard will Gigantamax his Urshifu at the first opportunity. After completing the Galarian Star Tournament, his Pokémon's levels will increase. Mustard uses these three Pokémon as both a partner and potential opponent in the Galarian Star Tournament. Mustard gives this Kubfu to the player once they have cleared his three trials. Mustard also owns three fast Galarian Slowpoke that are used in the first trial of the Master Dojo. All three Slowpoke have 31 IVs in HP and Speed, with 252 EVs in HP and Speed and 6 EVs in Special Defense. They also start the battle with a +6 stage boost in Speed. While these Slowpoke are treated as wild Pokémon encounters, none of the three can be caught. Mustard appeared in The Gathering of Stars, where he was seen in the Master Dojo at the Isle of Armor. He was visited by his student Leon, who gave him an invitation letter for the Galarian Star Tournament after having a quick battle with him. He was later seen at Wyndon Stadium, walking out onto the field for the tournament's opening ceremony. This listing is of Mustard's known Pokémon in Pokémon: Twilight Wings. Mustard debuted in Flutter!! Tickets to a New Land. While training, Klara and Avery found an unconscious Henry Sword suffering from the effects of Eternatus's poison. Henry was taken to the Master Dojo to recover and was given permission to stay as long as he wanted. Klara went off to greet a pair of new arrivals at the Armor Station, but returned with Casey and Marvin, who quickly clarified that they were just looking for Henry. While the three stayed on the island, Mustard lent Henry his Kubfu, hoping that traveling around would help Kubfu see the world and overcome its shyness. In Bom Bom!! Kubfu and Intense Training, while searching for the Rusted Sword and Shield, Henry, Casey, and Marvin arrived at the Tower of Darkness. A Master Dojo Student guarding the entrance barred the three from investigating the building unless Henry and Kubfu defeated her in a one-on-one battle first. Although he believed they weren't ready yet, Mustard allowed Henry and Kubfu to battle the student, but despite its best efforts, Kubfu was unable to win. Later that night, Kubfu went to search the tower by itself, only to be taken out by an unseen Pokémon. In Rrmbll!! Serious Tower Battle, after several days of training, Kubfu decided to instead train at the Tower of Waters. Henry and Kubfu battled through multiple floors until they reached the top, where Mustard and his Single Strike Style Urshifu were waiting. Urshifu proved to be a powerful opponent, but by targeting its feet, Kubfu forced Urshifu to stumble, leaving wide open to be defeated by a powerful Close Combat. Mustard allowed Kubfu to examine the Scroll of Waters, causing it to evolve into a Rapid Strike Style Urshifu. With their training complete, Mustard suggested Henry permanently keep Urshifu on his team, which he accepted. The following is a list of cards mentioning or featuring Mustard or his Pokémon in the Pokémon Trading Card Game. Mustard is the first NPC Champion to use a Legendary Pokémon in the core series games. Mustard is one of two characters to have a single digit uniform number, the other being Leon. Mustard is the only Champion who was a type specialist to not be a type specialist as a Champion. Despite not being a type specialist anymore, all the Pokémon that Mustard uses in the Galarian Star Tournament are of the Fighting type, possibly as a reference to his old specialty type.

Title: Sweet Herba Mystica

Sweet Herba Mystica (Japanese: ひでん：あまスパイス Secret: Sweet Spice) is a type of ingredient introduced in Generation IX. It is one of the five Herba Mystica. It can be used as an ingredient in making a sandwich. In Fly! Wattrel!!, Murdock bought different kinds of Herba Mystica in Levincia. In Acquisition! New Ability: Dash!!, Sweet Herba Mystica was the first Herba Mystica discovered by Violet and Arven after being led to it by Titan Klawf. After Klawf had been driven off, Arven made sandwiches with the Herba Mystica for himself, Violet, and Nemona. When Miraidon consumed one of the sandwiches, it gained the ability to dash faster.

Title: Salty Herba Mystica

Salty Herba Mystica (Japanese: ひでん：しおスパイス Secret: Salt Spice) is a type of ingredient introduced in Generation IX. It is one of the five Herba Mystica. It can be used as an ingredient in making a sandwich. In Fly! Wattrel!!, Murdock bought different kinds of Herba Mystica in Levincia. In PASV18, Salty Herba Mystica was the third Herba Mystica discovered by Violet and Arven after being led to it by Titan Orthworm. After Orthworm had been driven off, Arven made sandwiches with the Herba Mystica for himself, Violet, Scarlet, and Nemona. When Miraidon consumed one of the sandwiches, it gained the ability to jump higher. The Salty Herba Mystica is the only Herba Mystica to not share the same naming convention as the flavors used in Contests, where it would be referred to as "Dry"; rather, the flavors are the ones associated with the Chinese Five Phases.

Title: Sour Herba Mystica

Sour Herba Mystica (Japanese: ひでん：すぱスパイス Secret: Sour Spice) is a type of ingredient introduced in Generation IX. It is one of the five Herba Mystica. It can be used as an ingredient in making a sandwich. In Fly! Wattrel!!, Murdock bought different kinds of Herba Mystica in Levincia. In PASV25, Sour Herba Mystica was the fourth Herba Mystica discovered by Violet and Arven after being led to it by Titan Iron Treads and Great Tusk.

Title: Bitter Herba Mystica

Bitter Herba Mystica (Japanese: ひでん：にがスパイス Secret: Bitter Spice) is a type of ingredient introduced in Generation IX. It is one of the five Herba Mystica. It can be used as an ingredient in making a sandwich. In Fly! Wattrel!!, Murdock bought different kinds of Herba Mystica in Levincia. In PASV11, Violet and Arven discovered the Bitter Herba Mystica after defeating Titan Bombirdier. Arven made some sandwiches out of it, sharing them amongst himself, his Mabosstiff, Violet, and Mireia, a Musician who had helped them against Bombirdier. The power of the Bitter Herba Mystica allowed Mabosstiff to recover a little more, much to Arven's relief. When Miraidon ate one of the sandwiches, it gained a power-up, with Professor Turo contacting Violet to specify that Miraidon had regained its ability to swim.

Title: Spicy Herba Mystica

Spicy Herba Mystica (Japanese: ひでん：からスパイス Secret: Spicy Spice) is a type of ingredient introduced in Generation IX. It is one of the five Herba Mystica. It can be used as an ingredient in making a sandwich. In Fly! Wattrel!!, Liko received a supposed advertisement for Spicy Herba Mystica being sold in Levincia and left to get some for Murdock, but it turned out to be a lie created by Spinel to lure her into a trap.

Title: Gimmighoul Coin

The Gimmighoul Coin (Japanese: コレクレーのコイン Collecurei Coin) is a type of item introduced in Generation IX. In Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, it is treated as a TM Material. Having 999 Gimmighoul Coins causes Gimmighoul to evolve into Gholdengo when it levels up (or, in Pokémon Legends: Z-A, is manually evolved), consuming the coins in the process. Despite being categorized as a TM Material in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, Gimmighoul Coins are not actually used to make any TMs. In Pokémon GO, Gimmighoul Coins are used to evolve Gimmighoul into Gholdengo. Unlike other Evolution items, Gimmighoul Coins do not take up space in the player's Item Bag, and they cannot be discarded. This item was originally called the ?????? Coin (Japanese: ??????コイン ?????? Coin) prior to February 27, 2023. It first became available on November 5, 2022, immediately following Community Day Classic: Dratini, and a day before Gimmighoul's name was officially revealed. A player can evolve a Gimmighoul into Gholdengo if they have at least 999 Gimmighoul Coins in their Item Bag. Candy is not used for this Evolution, making Gimmighoul the only Pokémon in the game that evolves without Candy. Prior to February 27, 2023, as the ?????? Coin, this item was categorized as a Key Item and served no purpose in-game. Collecting one from a golden PokéStop caused a Roaming Form Gimmighoul to appear in the overworld, but it could not be caught until February 27, 2023. For the repeatable methods listed, the number of coins received and whether any coins are received at all are random, unless noted otherwise. In Pokémon Masters EX, Gimmighoul Coins are an event-exclusive item that could be collected during the special event Mysterious Old Coins and by completing the Get Gimmighoul Coins Mission Bingo. They could be exchanged in the Gimmighoul Coin Special Exchange for items. In Pokémon Café ReMix, Gimmighoul Coins are an event-exclusive item that can be obtained through Cook and Collect feature. Available since March 01, 2024, the "Collect with Gholdengo" event is active for periods of 4-5 days during which the player has to collect Gimmighoul coins in order to recruit Gholdengo. The coins are obtained by clearing Gimmighoul icons in the eligible puzzles, which at first included One-Minute Cooking and Slow Cooking events, but were later limited to Main Orders, Extra Orders and Master Café Mode starting on September 18, 2025. Clearing Roaming Form icons gives the player 1 coin; clearing Chest Form icons gives 5 coins. Only up to 50 coins can be collected per day. A total of 4,995 coins can be collected to unlock all the rewards from this event, which include Gholdengo, its outfit upgrades and all the Gholdengo cookies needed to unlock all its levels. Gimmighoul Coins first appeared in The Terapagos I Don't Know. One was first discovered by Ludlow, but was snatched from him almost immediately. Later, Roy collected a coin dropped by a Roaming Form Gimmighoul and went on collecting coins after being manipulated by a Chest Form Gimmighoul, who then took the coins and left. With the addition of the same Chest Form Gimmighoul to the wild Pokémon roster living aboard the Brave Olivine in The Knight in the Ruins and, later, to Dot's team in Riding the Waves, Gimmighoul Coins became a recurring item in the series. In HZ120, after it finally managed to gather 1,000 coins, Dot's Gimmighoul evolved into Gholdengo. A Gimmighoul Coin appeared in Breathe Out, where it was taken away by a Corviknight. A Gimmighoul Coin first appeared in Raid! The Dark Crew Base!!, where Scarlet was revealed to be collecting them as part of her profession as a treasure hunter. In Skillful! The Mysterious Treasure Hunter!!, Scarlet defeated a Chest Form Gimmighoul at a watchtower at South Province (Area Two) and collected 60 coins from it, bringing her total up to 88. In a flashback in Acquisition! New Ability: Dash!!, Scarlet collected more coins from another Chest Form Gimmighoul at South Province (Area Three).

Title: Auspicious Armor

The Auspicious Armor (Japanese: イワイノヨロイ Auspicious Armor) is a type of item introduced in Generation IX. Causes Charcadet to evolve into Armarouge upon use. This consumes the Auspicious Armor. A set of Auspicious Armor first appeared in HZ123, being given to Liko by her grandmother in order for her to evolve her Charcadet. In the following episode, Charcadet wore the armor during a battle against Indi and Rubina's two Salamence alongside Amethio's Ceruledge. During the climax of the battle, the armor triggered Charcadet's Evolution into Armarouge. A set of Auspicious Armor appeared in PT16 in Yūto's home. It was passed down in Yūto's family for generations, with a legend saying it was related to Charcadet. After hearing it, Yūto's Charcadet felt compelled to touch the armor and evolved into Armarouge as a result. A set of Auspicious Armor appeared in a flashback in Challenge! The Avant-Garde Gym Leader!!, where it was given to Scarlet by Mela, along with a Charcadet. She later used it to evolve Charcadet into Armarouge. Unlike with other Pokémon that have evolved using Evolution items in the animated series, Liko's Charcadet did not glow during its Evolution sequence; the Auspicious Armor did, instead.

Title: White Dish

The White Dish (Japanese: ホワイトプレート White Plate) is a type of picnic item introduced in Generation IX. It is currently unobtainable. The White Dish serves no practical purpose other than to decorate the player's picnic.

Title: Yellow Dish

The Yellow Dish (Japanese: イエロープレート Yellow Plate) is a type of picnic item introduced in Generation IX. It is currently unobtainable. The Yellow Dish serves no practical purpose other than to decorate the player's picnic.

Title: Teal Style Card

If you were looking for the Key Item from The Isle of Armor, see Style Card. The Teal Style Card (Japanese: おしゃれカードみどり Turquoise Fashion Card) is a Key Item introduced in Generation IX. The Teal Style Card gives the player more options at certain clothing stores and at hair salons.

Title: Teal Mask

This article is about the Key Item. For the expansion for Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, see The Teal Mask. The Teal Mask (Japanese: みどりのめん Green Mask) is a Key Item introduced in Generation IX, and the titular item of The Teal Mask. The Teal Mask is to be returned to Ogerpon as part of the plot. Ogerpon wears the Teal Mask in its Teal Mask form, which is its default form when it is not holding the Wellspring Mask, Hearthflame Mask, or Cornerstone Mask. Main article: Ogre's Mask (Twilight Masquerade 159) The Teal Mask is depicted alongside the rest of Ogerpon's masks in the Ogre's Mask card, which was introduced as an Item card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game during the English Scarlet & Violet Series (the Japanese Scarlet & Violet Era). It was released in the Japanese Transformation Mask expansion and the English Twilight Masquerade expansion, with artwork by 5ban Graphics. It allows the player to search their discard pile for a Pokémon ex card with "Ogerpon" in its name, and switch it with another "Ogerpon"-named Pokémon ex card already in play, essentially "changing" its form.

Title: Glimmering Charm

The Glimmering Charm (Japanese: きらめくおまもり Sparkling Charm) is a Key Item introduced in Generation IX. Upon completion of a Tera Raid Battle, the Glittering Charm adds an extra item slot with Tera Shards of the Raid's type to the reward list. The number of Shards in this slot depends on the Raid's difficulty: The Glimmering Charm has no effect on 1★, 2★, or 7★ Tera Raids.

Title: Crystal Cluster

The Crystal Cluster (Japanese: けっしょうのかけら Crystal Fragment) is a Key Item introduced in Generation IX. The Crystal Cluster is used by Kieran and Carmine's grandpa to repair Ogerpon's Teal Mask.

Title: Fairy Feather

Fairy Feather (Japanese: ようせいのハネ Fairy Feather) is a type of held item introduced in Generation IX that boosts the power of Fairy-type moves. Boosts the power of the holder's Fairy-type moves by 20%. The Fairy Feather is the last non-consumable type-enhancing item to be introduced that is not related to a specific Pokémon, such as Plates. Prior to the introduction of the Fairy Feather in The Teal Mask, the Fairy Gem and Pixie Plate were introduced as held items that boost the power of Fairy-type attacks, but only the latter has ever been made obtainable.

Title: Wellspring Mask

The Wellspring Mask (Japanese: いどのめん Well Mask) is a type of held item introduced in Generation IX. It changes Ogerpon to its Wellspring Mask form while held. If an Ogerpon is holding a Wellspring Mask, it changes to its Wellspring Mask form. It increases the power of its moves by 20%. Main article: Ogre's Mask (Twilight Masquerade 159) The Wellspring Mask is depicted alongside the rest of Ogerpon's masks in the Ogre's Mask card, which was introduced as an Item card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game during the English Scarlet & Violet Series (the Japanese Scarlet & Violet Era). It was released in the Japanese Transformation Mask expansion and the English Twilight Masquerade expansion, with artwork by 5ban Graphics. It allows the player to search their discard pile for a Pokémon ex card with "Ogerpon" in its name, and switch it with another "Ogerpon"-named Pokémon ex card already in play, essentially "changing" its form.

Title: Hearthflame Mask

The Hearthflame Mask (Japanese: かまどのめん Kamado Mask) is a type of held item introduced in Generation IX. It changes Ogerpon to its Hearthflame Mask form while held. If an Ogerpon is holding a Hearthflame Mask, it changes to its Hearthflame Mask form. It increases the power of its moves by 20%. Main article: Ogre's Mask (Twilight Masquerade 159) The Hearthflame Mask is depicted alongside the rest of Ogerpon's masks in the Ogre's Mask card, which was introduced as an Item card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game during the English Scarlet & Violet Series (the Japanese Scarlet & Violet Era). It was released in the Japanese Transformation Mask expansion and the English Twilight Masquerade expansion, with artwork by 5ban Graphics. It allows the player to search their discard pile for a Pokémon ex card with "Ogerpon" in its name, and switch it with another "Ogerpon"-named Pokémon ex card already in play, essentially "changing" its form.

Title: Cornerstone Mask

The Cornerstone Mask (Japanese: いしずえのめん Cornerstone Mask) is a type of held item introduced in Generation IX. It changes Ogerpon to its Cornerstone Mask form while held. If an Ogerpon is holding a Cornerstone Mask, it changes to its Cornerstone Mask form. It increases the power of its moves by 20%. Main article: Ogre's Mask (Twilight Masquerade 159) The Cornerstone Mask is depicted alongside the rest of Ogerpon's masks in the Ogre's Mask card, which was introduced as an Item card in the Pokémon Trading Card Game during the English Scarlet & Violet Series (the Japanese Scarlet & Violet Era). It was released in the Japanese Transformation Mask expansion and the English Twilight Masquerade expansion, with artwork by 5ban Graphics. It allows the player to search their discard pile for a Pokémon ex card with "Ogerpon" in its name, and switch it with another "Ogerpon"-named Pokémon ex card already in play, essentially "changing" its form.

Title: Syrupy Apple

The Syrupy Apple (Japanese: みついりりんご Honeycore Apple) is a type of item introduced in Generation IX. Causes Applin to evolve into Dipplin upon use. This consumes the Syrupy Apple. Syrupy Apples were added to Pokémon GO on October 10, 2025. Syrupy Apples are required to evolve certain Pokémon in Pokémon GO. They are consumed upon use. Applin requires 200 and 20 Syrupy Apples to evolve into Dipplin. Tapping apples that appear near PokéStops can award either Sweet Apples, Tart Apples, Syrupy Apples, or an encounter with a wild Applin. Apples will also have an increased chance of appearing at PokéStops with Mossy Lure Modules.

Title: Unremarkable Teacup

The Unremarkable Teacup (Japanese: ボンサクのちゃわん Mediocre Tea Bowl) is a type of item introduced in Generation IX. Causes Counterfeit Form Poltchageist to evolve into Unremarkable Form Sinistcha upon use. This consumes the item.

Title: Masterpiece Teacup

The Masterpiece Teacup (Japanese: ケッサクのちゃわん Masterpiece Tea Bowl) is a type of item introduced in Generation IX. Causes Artisan Form Poltchageist to evolve into Masterpiece Form Sinistcha upon use. This consumes the item.

Title: Health Mochi

The Health Mochi (Japanese: たいりょくのもち Stamina Mochi) is a type of item introduced in Generation IX. It is a type of mochi that increases the EVs of one of a Pokémon's stats. If used from the Bag on a Pokémon, it increases that Pokémon's HP EVs by 10.

Title: Muscle Mochi

The Muscle Mochi (Japanese: きんりょくのもち Muscle Mochi) is a type of item introduced in Generation IX. It is a type of mochi that increases the EVs of one of a Pokémon's stats. If used from the Bag on a Pokémon, it increases that Pokémon's Attack EVs by 10.

Title: Resist Mochi

The Resist Mochi (Japanese: ていこうのもち Resistance Mochi) is a type of item introduced in Generation IX. It is a type of mochi that increases the EVs of one of a Pokémon's stats. If used from the Bag on a Pokémon, it increases that Pokémon's Defense EVs by 10.

Title: Genius Mochi

The Genius Mochi (Japanese: ちりょくのもち Intelligence Mochi) is a type of item introduced in Generation IX. It is a type of mochi that increases the EVs of one of a Pokémon's stats. If used from the Bag on a Pokémon, it increases that Pokémon's Sp. Atk EVs by 10.

Title: Clever Mochi

The Clever Mochi (Japanese: せいしんのもち Mind Mochi) is a type of item introduced in Generation IX. It is a type of mochi that increases the EVs of one of a Pokémon's stats. If used from the Bag on a Pokémon, it increases that Pokémon's Sp. Def EVs by 10.

Title: Swift Mochi

The Swift Mochi (Japanese: しゅんぱつのもち Instant Mochi) is a type of item introduced in Generation IX. It is a type of mochi that increases the EVs of one of a Pokémon's stats. If used from the Bag on a Pokémon, it increases that Pokémon's Speed EVs by 10.

Title: Linking Cord

The Linking Cord (Japanese: つながりのヒモ Linking Cord) is a type of Evolution item introduced in Pokémon Legends: Arceus. It allows Kadabra, Machoke, Graveler, and Haunter to evolve into Alakazam, Machamp, Golem, and Gengar, respectively. The Linking Cord triggers the Evolution of certain Pokémon (all of which can alternatively evolve when traded) upon use. This consumes the Linking Cord. The Linking Cord is required to evolve certain Pokémon in Pokémon Sleep. It is consumed upon use. Graveler requires 80 Geodude Candy and a Linking Cord to evolve into Golem. Slowpoke requires 80 Slowpoke Candy, a Linking Cord, and a King's Rock to evolve into Slowking. Haunter requires 80 Gastly Candy and a Linking Cord to evolve into Gengar. Onix requires 80 Onix Candy, a Linking Cord, and a Metal Coat to evolve into Steelix. As of version 1.0.13, if the Linking Cord is used from the Bag, it will reward the player with 5 candies of a random Pokémon that evolves using the Linking Cord. While the Linking Cord is used to evolve Pokémon that evolve through trading, it cannot be used to evolve Pokémon that evolve by trading with a held item; instead, in Pokémon Legends: Arceus, those Pokémon evolve simply by using that item on them. This item is a reference to the Game Link Cable, which is used to trade Pokémon (and consequently activate trade Evolutions) in the first three generations of core series games (outside of trading using the Game Boy Advance Wireless Adapter). The sprite of the Linking Cord in Pokémon Legends: Arceus takes influence from a form of Japanese knot tying called Mizuhiki. The knot in particular is an Awaji knot, which is used to represent togetherness — a possible nod to the Link Cable's original use. There is an Evolution item in the first three games of the Pokémon Mystery Dungeon series called Link Cable, which works similarly to the Linking Cord but predates its introduction.

Title: Black Augurite

The Black Augurite (Japanese: くろのきせき Black Augite) is a type of item introduced in Pokémon Legends: Arceus. It allows Scyther to evolve into Kleavor. Causes Scyther to evolve into Kleavor upon use. This consumes the Black Augurite. Despite being unobtainable in Pokémon Scarlet and Violet, the Terarium has a deposit of Black Augurite in the Canyon Biome, with Kleavor and Scyther being able to spawn around it.

Title: Peat Block

The Peat Block (Japanese: ピートブロック Peat Block) is a type of item introduced in Pokémon Legends: Arceus. It allows Ursaring to evolve into Ursaluna. Causes Ursaring to evolve into Ursaluna when used during a full moon. This consumes the Peat Block.
